CodeTermOne-liner
Block 0 · PC Basics
B·01HardwarePhysical parts of a computer
B·02CPUThe brain — executes instructions
B·03RAMTemporary memory used while running programs
B·04SoftwarePrograms that run on hardware
B·05Operating SystemSoftware that manages everything else
B·06Binary & HexThe number systems computers actually use
B·07File SystemHow the OS organizes and stores files
B·08PathThe address of a file inside the system
B·09PermissionsWho can read, write or run a file
B·10User & AdminRegular user vs full-control user
B·11Terminal / CLIText interface to control the system
B·12Environment VariablesSystem-wide values programs can read
B·13ProcessA program currently running
B·14Port (local)Channel a program listens on
B·QRQuick ReferenceKey terms, commands & reminders
B·MMMind MapVisual summaries of every term
B·MNMy NotesFree space for discoveries & questions
Block 1 · How the Internet Works
W·01Internet vs WebNot the same thing
W·02IP AddressUnique address of a device on a network
W·03DNSTranslates domain names into IP addresses
W·04HTTP / HTTPSProtocol for loading web pages
W·05URLThe full address of a web resource
W·06Request & ResponseHow browser and server talk
W·07Status CodesServer reply codes (200, 404, 500…)
W·08HeadersMetadata sent with every request
W·09CookiesSmall files stored to remember the user
W·10SessionsServer-side way to keep a user logged in
W·11APIInterface for programs to talk to each other
W·12Client & ServerWho asks, who answers
W·13CDNDistributed servers that speed up delivery
W·14SSL / TLSEncryption layer that makes HTTPS work
W·QRQuick ReferenceStatus codes, headers & URL anatomy
W·MMMind MapVisual summaries of every term
W·MNMy NotesFree space for discoveries & questions
Block 2 · Networks
N·01NetworkGroup of connected devices
N·02LAN vs WANLocal vs wide area network
N·03OSI Model7-layer framework for understanding networks
N·04TCPReliable, ordered packet delivery
N·05UDPFaster, connectionless alternative to TCP
N·06MAC AddressPhysical hardware address of a device
N·07RouterConnects different networks together
N·08SwitchConnects devices within the same network
N·09FirewallFilters traffic by rules
N·10Ports & ProtocolsWhich door each service uses
N·11PacketUnit of data traveling through a network
N·12BandwidthMaximum data transfer capacity
N·13ProxyIntermediary between client and server
N·14VPNEncrypted tunnel over the internet
N·15NATTranslates private IPs to public ones
N·16SubnetDivision of a network into smaller parts
N·17DHCPAutomatically assigns IP addresses
Block 3 · Linux
L·01LinuxOpen-source OS used in most security tools
L·02DistroA version/flavor of Linux
L·03Shell / BashThe terminal language
L·04File System StructureHow Linux organizes folders
L·05Basic Commandsls, cd, cat, mkdir, rm, cp, mv
L·06File Permissionsrwx — who can do what to a file
L·07Users & GroupsHow Linux manages access by identity
L·08Root & SudoSuperuser and how to borrow its power
L·09GrepSearch for patterns inside files
L·10Pipe & RedirectConnect commands and save output
L·11ProcessesRunning programs and how to manage them
L·12SSHRemote access to another machine securely
L·13Package ManagerHow to install software (apt, brew…)
L·14CronSchedule tasks to run automatically
L·15Log FilesSystem records of what happened and when
Block 4 · Programming Basics
P·01Programming LogicThinking like a machine — step by step.
P·02VariableA named container for data.
P·03FunctionReusable block of logic with a name.
P·04LoopRepeating work until done.
P·05ConditionMaking decisions in code.
P·06Array / ListAn ordered collection of values.
P·07DictionaryKey-value mapping for fast lookup.
P·08String ManipulationWorking with text.
P·09File I/OReading from and writing to disk.
P·10Python for HackersWhy Python is the hacker's language.
Block 5 · Security Fundamentals
S·01CIA TriadThe three pillars of every security decision.
S·02ThreatA potential cause of harm.
S·03VulnerabilityA weakness that can be exploited.
S·04ExploitWeaponizing a vulnerability.
S·05RiskThe chance and impact of something going wrong.
S·06Attack SurfaceEverything exposed that could be targeted.
S·07Defense in DepthLayered defense — if one fails, the next catches it.
S·08Zero TrustTrust nothing. Verify everything.
S·09EncryptionMaking data unreadable to anyone without the key.
S·10HashingA one-way fingerprint of data.
S·11AuthenticationProving who you are.
S·12AuthorizationDeciding what you're allowed to do.
Block 6 · Web Attacks
A·01SQL InjectionInjecting SQL into a query that wasn't supposed to take any.
A·02XSSInjecting JavaScript that runs in another user's browser.
A·03CSRFTricking a logged-in user into doing actions without consent.
A·04SSRFMaking the server send requests on the attacker's behalf.
A·05Path TraversalEscaping the intended directory with ../ sequences.
A·06Command InjectionInjecting OS commands into a system call.
A·07IDORInsecure Direct Object Reference — changing an ID, seeing someone else's data.
A·08Open RedirectSending users to attacker-controlled URLs via your domain.
A·09ClickjackingTricking the user into clicking something they didn't see.
A·10Session HijackingStealing or guessing a session token to impersonate a user.
A·11Authentication BypassLogging in without valid credentials.
A·12Race ConditionTwo operations colliding in time to break a check.
Block 7 · Pentest Tools
T·01nmapNetwork mapper, port scanner, service enumerator.
T·02Burp SuiteWeb proxy for intercepting/modifying HTTP traffic.
T·03WiresharkNetwork protocol analyzer.
T·04MetasploitExploit framework with payloads and listeners.
T·05sqlmapAutomated SQL injection scanner and exploiter.
T·06Gobuster / DirbDirectory and file enumeration via wordlist brute force.
T·07John the RipperCPU-based password cracker for hashes and encrypted files.
T·08HashcatGPU-accelerated password cracker — the fastest in the world.
T·09HydraNetwork login brute-forcer for SSH, FTP, HTTP forms and more.
T·10netcatSwiss army knife of networking — listener, scanner, file transfer.
T·11curlCLI HTTP client for testing APIs and crafting requests.
T·12Browser DevToolsInspecting requests, cookies, JS and storage — built into every browser.
Block 8 · Pentest Methodology
M·01ReconnaissancePassive and active gathering of target intelligence.
M·02ScanningPort, service and version discovery on live targets.
M·03EnumerationExtracting usernames, shares, banners and structural info.
M·04ExploitationTriggering a vulnerability to gain unauthorized access.
M·05Privilege EscalationMoving from low-privilege user to root or SYSTEM.
M·06PersistenceMaintaining access through reboots, patches and detection sweeps.
M·07Lateral MovementPivoting from one compromised host to others.
M·08ExfiltrationStealing data from the target via covert channels.
M·09Covering TracksClearing logs and removing artifacts to delay detection.
M·10ReportingDocumenting findings for the client — the actual product.
Block 9 · Social Engineering
E·01PhishingMass email to trick clicks, credentials or attachment execution.
E·02Spear PhishingTargeted phishing with personalized lure for one person or small group.
E·03PretextingFabricated scenario to extract information or trigger action.
E·04BaitingPhysical or digital lure — USB drops, fake downloads, free stuff.
E·05TailgatingPhysical entry by following an authorized person through a door.
E·06VishingVoice phishing — social engineering over phone calls.
E·07SmishingSMS phishing — phishing via text messages.
E·08Watering HoleCompromising websites the target population visits.
E·09Quid Pro QuoOffering something in exchange for information or access.
E·10ImpersonationPretending to be someone of authority — IT, executive, vendor, regulator.
Block 10 · Malware
X·01VirusSelf-replicating code that infects host files.
X·02WormSelf-propagating malware; spreads across networks without a host.
X·03TrojanDisguised as legit software; payload activates on execution.
X·04RansomwareEncrypts data; demands ransom for the decryption key.
X·05SpywareCovert surveillance — keys, screen, mic, browser data.
X·06RootkitHides at OS / kernel level to maintain stealth access.
X·07KeyloggerRecords every keystroke — hardware or software.
X·08BackdoorHidden access mechanism bypassing normal authentication.
X·09BotnetNetwork of compromised devices controlled remotely.
X·10Logic BombTriggers on specific event, date, or condition.
Block 11 · Defense
D·01FirewallFilters traffic by rules — stateful vs stateless.
D·02IDS / IPSIntrusion Detection (passive) and Prevention (blocks) Systems.
D·03SIEMCentralized log aggregation, correlation, and alerting.
D·04EDREndpoint Detection & Response — visibility, hunt, respond.
D·05WAFWeb Application Firewall — Layer 7 filtering.
D·06HoneypotDecoy systems to attract and study attackers.
D·07Patch ManagementTracking and deploying vendor fixes — KEV / CVSS / EPSS.
D·08Backup Strategy3-2-1 rule, offline backups, restore testing.
D·09Incident ResponsePrep / Identify / Contain / Eradicate / Recover / Lessons.
D·10Security Awareness TrainingHuman-firewall: phishing tests + education.
Back of Book · Master Quick References
QR·01Ports CheatsheetMost important ports and what uses them
QR·02HTTP Status CodesAll codes grouped and explained
QR·03Linux CommandsCommands you will use every single day
QR·04OSI LayersEach layer, its job and an example
QR·05Attack to Cause to FixEvery web attack in one table
QR·06Pentest ChecklistPhase by phase — what to do in what order
QR·07Malware ID GuideType to behavior to detection clue
QR·08Tools at a GlanceTool to what it does to basic command
cherry root › glossary
Personal Glossary
One-liner definitions for fast memory triggers. Alphabetical order. All blocks covered.
A
ACLAccess Control List — rules defining who can access what resource
algorithmStep-by-step set of instructions a computer follows
APIApplication Programming Interface — lets programs talk to each other
ASCIIStandard encoding that maps text characters to numbers
asymmetric encryptionUses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt
attack surfaceEverything exposed that could be targeted by an attacker
authenticationProving who you are — login, password, biometrics
authorizationWhat you are allowed to do after authenticating
argon2idModern password-hashing KDF — winner of the Password Hashing Competition
arrayOrdered collection of values accessed by numeric index
atomic operationOperation that completes fully or not at all — no half states visible
authentication bypassLogging in without valid credentials via logic flaws or default creds
B
backdoorHidden access mechanism that bypasses normal authentication
botnetNetwork of compromised devices remotely controlled via a C2 server
bootkitRootkit that infects the boot loader (MBR/UEFI) and loads before the OS
baitingLeaving attractive bait (USB sticks, downloads) hoping a victim runs it
BadUSBMalicious device disguised as a USB stick that injects keystrokes on plug-in
Burp SuiteIndustry-standard web proxy for intercepting and modifying HTTP traffic
BloodHoundAD attack-path visualization tool for finding shortest path to Domain Admin
backdoorHidden access left open in a system for future entry
baitingLeaving something tempting (USB, link) to trick a victim
bandwidthMaximum data transfer capacity of a connection
bashMost common shell on Linux — interprets your commands
binaryBase-2 number system — only 0s and 1s
bitSmallest unit of data — a single 0 or 1
blue teamTeam responsible for defense, monitoring and incident response
bootloaderSoftware that loads the OS when you power on
botnetNetwork of infected machines under attacker control
broken authExploiting weak login or session logic to gain access
bufferTemporary memory area for data in transit
bug bountyProgram that pays researchers to find real vulnerabilities
byte8 bits — smallest addressable unit of memory
bcryptAdaptive password-hashing function — slow on purpose to resist GPUs
C
C2Command and Control — the server an attacker uses to control malware / bots
CISA KEVCISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog — patch with priority
Cobalt StrikeCommercial red-team C2 framework heavily abused by ransomware operators
covering tracksErasing forensic evidence of an intrusion — logs, history, artifacts
curlCommand-line HTTP client for crafting requests and testing APIs
C2 serverCommand and Control — attacker center for managing the botnet
CDNContent Delivery Network — distributed servers that speed up delivery
certificateDigital document proving identity on the web — used in HTTPS
chmodLinux command to change file permissions
CIA TriadConfidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three pillars of security
CLICommand Line Interface — interacting with a system via text commands
clientThe device or program that sends a request to a server
closed portA port not accepting connections — not listening
command injectionExecuting system commands through unsanitized user input
cookieSmall file stored in the browser to remember user state
core (CPU)Individual processing unit inside a CPU — more cores equals more parallel work
covering tracksRemoving logs and evidence of an intrusion
CPUCentral Processing Unit — the brain that executes all instructions
CSRFCross-Site Request Forgery — tricking a user into making unwanted requests
CTFCapture The Flag — legal hacking competition to practice skills
CVECommon Vulnerabilities and Exposures — unique ID for a known vulnerability
cryptographyTurning data into unreadable form to protect it from unauthorized access
clickjackingTricking users into clicking hidden UI by overlaying invisible frames
conditionBranching point in code — if/elif/else evaluating boolean expressions
CSPContent Security Policy — HTTP header restricting which scripts/resources can load
CSRFCross-Site Request Forgery — abusing logged-in browsers to perform unwanted actions
D
DevToolsBuilt-in browser developer tools (F12) for inspecting requests, DOM, storage, JS
deepfakeAI-generated voice or video impersonating a real person convincingly
DHCPDynamic Host Configuration Protocol — automatically assigns IP addresses
distroA version and flavor of Linux (e.g. Kali, Ubuntu, Debian)
DNSDomain Name System — translates domain names into IP addresses
dropperMalware whose purpose is to install other malware
defense in depthLayered security — overlapping controls so no single failure equals a breach
dictionaryKey-value mapping for fast lookup — Python dict / hash table
E
EDREndpoint Detection & Response — telemetry-rich successor to antivirus
EmotetNotorious banking trojan / loader botnet, taken down 2021, returned later
EternalBlueLeaked NSA SMBv1 exploit (CVE-2017-0144) used by WannaCry and NotPetya
EvilginxReal-time MitM phishing framework that captures session cookies to bypass MFA
enumerationTargeted extraction of usernames, shares, banners and details from discovered services
exfiltrationMoving stolen data from target network to attacker infrastructure
exploitationTriggering a vulnerability to gain initial unauthorized access
encryptionTransforming data so only authorized parties can read it
env variableSystem-wide value stored in memory that any program can read
executableA file the OS can run as a program (binary)
exploitCode or technique that abuses a vulnerability to gain access
exploitationThe phase of using a vulnerability to actually gain access
ext4Default Linux file system format
exploit (entry)Code or technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability
F
firewallFilters network traffic by rules — stateful tracks connections, stateless per-packet
FAT32File system used on USB drives and older storage media
file systemSystem that organizes and addresses all files on disk
firewallFilters network traffic using rules — allows or blocks packets
firmwareLow-level software embedded directly into hardware
forensicsInvestigating what happened during or after a security incident
functionReusable block of code that performs a specific task
FIDO2Modern phishing-resistant auth standard using hardware security keys
file I/OReading from and writing to files on disk
functionReusable named block of code that takes inputs and returns output
G
GobusterFast Go-based brute-force tool for directories, files, vhosts and subdomains
grepLinux command to search for patterns inside files or output
H
honeypotDecoy system instrumented to detect and study attackers
honeytokenFake credential, file, or record that fires an alert when used
HashcatGPU-accelerated password cracker — fastest of its class
HydraNetwork login brute-forcer supporting 50+ protocols (SSH, FTP, RDP, HTTP)
hardeningReducing attack surface by removing weaknesses and unnecessary services
hardwarePhysical parts of a computer — keyboard, CPU, RAM, disk
hashOne-way fingerprint of data — cannot be reversed
heapPart of RAM used for dynamic memory allocation at runtime
hexHexadecimal — base-16 number system, shorthand for binary
honeypotFake system designed to attract and study attackers
HTTPHyperText Transfer Protocol — protocol for loading web pages
HTTPSHTTP with TLS encryption — the secure version of HTTP
I
IDSIntrusion Detection System — passive monitoring and alerting on attacks
IPSIntrusion Prevention System — inline, blocks attacks in real time
infostealerMalware that harvests saved passwords, cookies, and wallets (RedLine, Vidar, Lumma)
incident responseStructured handling of security incidents: PICERL — Prep, Identify, Contain, Eradicate, Recover, Lessons
impersonationAssuming another identity (IT, executive, vendor) to leverage trust and access
IDORInsecure Direct Object Reference — accessing another user data by changing an ID
IDSIntrusion Detection System — monitors and alerts on suspicious activity
impersonationPretending to be a trusted person to extract info or access
incident responseStructured process for handling and recovering from a security breach
IPSIntrusion Prevention System — detects and actively blocks suspicious activity
IP addressUnique numerical address of a device on a network
IDORInsecure Direct Object Reference — accessing others' data by changing an ID
J
John the RipperClassic CPU-based password cracker with hundreds of supported hash formats
K
keyloggerCaptures every keystroke — software (API hooks) or hardware (inline USB)
kernelCore of the OS — manages hardware resources directly
keyloggerMalware that records every keystroke the victim types
L
logic bombMalicious code that activates only when a specific condition is met
lateral movementPivoting from initial foothold to other hosts in the internal network
LANLocal Area Network — network within a limited physical area
least privilegeGiving only the minimum access needed — nothing more
log filesSystem records of events — what happened and when
loopCode structure that repeats a block of instructions (for, while)
LFILocal File Inclusion — reading server files via path traversal
listPython's ordered, mutable sequence — same as array in other languages
loopBlock of code that repeats — for or while in Python
M
MetasploitModular exploit framework standardizing exploit/payload/listener workflow
MeterpreterMetasploit's in-memory payload providing extensive post-exploitation capabilities
MAC addressPhysical hardware address permanently assigned to a network device
malwareAny software intentionally designed to cause harm
memory dumpSnapshot of RAM contents captured at a specific moment
MFAMulti-Factor Authentication — verifying identity with 2 or more factors
motherboardMain circuit board that connects all hardware components
mount pointLocation in the file system where a storage device is attached
N
netcatSwiss-army-knife TCP/UDP tool — listener, scanner, file transfer, reverse shell
nmapNetwork mapper for host, port, service and version discovery
NATNetwork Address Translation — maps private IPs to a public IP
networkGroup of connected devices that can communicate with each other
NTFSDefault Windows file system format
O
open portA port actively listening and accepting connections
OSOperating System — software that manages hardware and all other software
OSINTOpen Source Intelligence — gathering public info about a target
OSI model7-layer framework explaining how data travels through a network
OWASP Top 10List of the 10 most critical web application security risks
OAuthStandard protocol for delegated access — login with Google/GitHub
open redirectEndpoint that redirects to a user-controlled URL — abused for phishing
P
patch managementDisciplined process of testing and deploying vendor security fixes within an SLA
PegasusNSO Group commercial spyware with zero-click iOS/Android exploits
persistenceMaintaining access through reboots, patches and detection sweeps
pretextingFabricating a believable scenario to extract information or trigger action
privilege escalationMoving from low-privilege user to root or SYSTEM
packetSmall unit of data that travels independently through a network
pathFull address of a file or folder inside the file system
path traversalUsing ../ in input to escape allowed directories and read sensitive files
payloadThe malicious part of an attack — what actually executes
pentestAuthorized simulation of an attack to find vulnerabilities
peripheralExternal hardware connected to a computer (printer, mouse, USB)
permissionsRules that define who can read, write or execute a file
persistenceMaintaining access to a system even after reboot
phishingSending fake emails to trick users into giving up credentials
PIDProcess ID — unique number assigned to each running process
portNumbered channel a service listens on for incoming connections
post-exploitationActions taken after gaining access — moving laterally, escalating
pretextingCreating a fabricated scenario to extract sensitive info
priv escalationGoing from limited user access to full admin or root control
processA program currently being executed by the CPU with its own PID
process injectionEmbedding malicious code inside a legitimate running process
proxyIntermediary that sits between client and server — can intercept traffic
path traversalEscaping the intended directory using ../ sequences
PoCProof of Concept — minimal exploit demonstrating a vulnerability works
PythonHigh-level interpreted language — dominant in offensive tooling and CTFs
Q
quid pro quoSocial-engineering attack offering a benefit in exchange for information or access
R
ransomwareMalware that encrypts data and demands payment for decryption; often double-extorts via leaks
RATRemote Access Trojan — gives operator full control of the victim machine
rootkitMalware that hides at kernel / firmware level to evade detection
RPO / RTORecovery Point / Time Objective — acceptable data loss and downtime
reconnaissancePhase 1 of an attack — passive and active gathering of target intel
reportingFinal pentest phase — documenting findings, evidence and severity for the client
RAMRandom Access Memory — fast volatile memory used while programs run
ransomwareMalware that encrypts victim files and demands payment for decryption
reconReconnaissance — gathering information about a target before attacking
red teamTeam that simulates real attackers in a controlled security exercise
registersTiny ultra-fast memory inside the CPU for currently active data
rootSuperuser account with unrestricted access to the entire system
rootkitMalware that hides deep in the OS to avoid detection
routerNetwork device that connects different networks and routes traffic
rwxRead, write, execute — the three Linux permission types
RBACRole-Based Access Control — permissions granted by role membership
race conditionBug where timing/ordering of concurrent operations breaks a check
RCERemote Code Execution — running attacker code on the target system
riskLikelihood times impact — what to prioritize fixing
S
SIEMSecurity Information and Event Management — centralizes logs, correlates, alerts
spywareMalware focused on covert surveillance — keys, screen, mic, browser
SigmaVendor-neutral SIEM detection-rule language (YAML)
smishingSMS-based phishing — fraudulent text messages with malicious links
scanningProbing live hosts for open ports, services and software versions
spear phishingHighly personalized phishing targeting a specific individual based on OSINT
sqlmapAutomated SQL injection scanner and exploitation tool
scanningDiscovering open ports, services and vulnerabilities on a target
sensitive data exposureLeaving confidential data unprotected or unencrypted
serverThe device or program that listens for and responds to requests
sessionServer-side mechanism to keep track of a logged-in user
shellProgram that interprets and executes terminal commands
SIEMSecurity Information and Event Management — collects and correlates logs
smishingPhishing attacks conducted over SMS text messages
SOCSecurity Operations Center — centralized defense and monitoring hub
social engineeringManipulating people psychologically to extract info or access
softwareInstructions that tell hardware what to do — code with no physical form
spear phishingHighly targeted phishing using personal info about the victim
spywareMalware that secretly monitors and collects user data
SQL injectionInjecting SQL code into input fields to manipulate a database
SSRFServer-Side Request Forgery — making the server send requests on your behalf
SSD / HDDPersistent storage devices — data survives shutdown
SSL / TLSCryptographic protocols that secure HTTPS connections
stackPart of RAM used for function calls and local variable storage
status codeServer numeric reply code — 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Error
subnetA subdivision of a larger network into smaller logical segments
sudoLinux command to run a single command with root privileges
switchNetwork device that connects multiple devices within the same network
symmetric encryptionUses the same key to both encrypt and decrypt data
SameSiteCookie attribute controlling whether cookies travel on cross-site requests
session hijackingStealing or guessing a session token to impersonate a user
SQLiSQL Injection — making the database execute attacker-controlled queries
SSRFServer-Side Request Forgery — making the server fetch URLs the attacker chooses
stringSequence of characters — text data manipulated via slicing, regex, etc.
T
trojanMalware disguised as legitimate software; payload activates when user runs it
3-2-1 backup3 copies, 2 media types, 1 off-site — classic backup-resilience rule
tailgatingPhysically following someone into a restricted area without authorization
TCPTransmission Control Protocol — ensures reliable ordered packet delivery
terminalText interface to control the OS directly by typing commands
threat huntingProactively searching for hidden attackers already inside a network
threat modelMapping what could go wrong, why, and what to protect against
threadSmallest unit of execution within a process
trojanMalware disguised as legitimate software to trick users into running it
threatPotential cause of harm — actor, event or condition
threat modelStructured analysis of what could go wrong and where
TOCTOUTime-of-Check to Time-of-Use — race condition between check and action
U
UDPUser Datagram Protocol — faster than TCP but no delivery guarantee
URLUniform Resource Locator — full address of a web resource
userAccount with limited access on a system — opposite of admin or root
V
virusSelf-replicating malicious code that infects a host file and spreads when host runs
vishingVoice phishing — social engineering over phone calls
variableA named container that stores a value in a program
virusMalware that attaches itself to files and spreads when executed
vishingPhishing attacks conducted over voice calls
volatile memoryMemory that is completely erased when power is lost (RAM)
VPNVirtual Private Network — encrypted tunnel protecting traffic over internet
vulnerabilityA weakness in software, config or hardware that can be exploited
W
WAFWeb Application Firewall — Layer 7 filtering against SQLi, XSS, etc.
wormSelf-propagating malware that spreads across networks with no user interaction
WannaCry2017 ransomware worm using EternalBlue; hit ~200k machines in 150 countries
watering holeCompromising a legitimate site the target population visits
WiresharkNetwork protocol analyzer that captures and dissects packets in real time
WANWide Area Network — network spanning large geographic distances
wormSelf-spreading malware that needs no user interaction to propagate
X
XDRExtended Detection & Response — EDR plus email, identity, cloud signals
XSSCross-Site Scripting — injecting scripts into pages other users will see
Z
zero trustSecurity model — trust nothing by default, always verify
cherry root › study method
My Study System
How to use this grimoire to learn fast and retain everything.
Daily Session (1–2h)
30 min — read one term page
20 min — watch a short video on the topic
1h — practice on TryHackMe or lab
10 min — write what you learned, update glossary
Active Recall (after every term)
Close the grimoire
Write everything you remember on a blank page
Open and check — note only what you forgot
Review the gaps, not everything again
Flashcards — Anki
After each page: create 3–5 cards with keywords
Review Anki every day for 10 minutes
Front: keyword · Back: one-line definition
Feynman Test (weekly)
Pick one topic from the week
Explain it out loud as if teaching a friend
Where you get stuck = what you do not know yet
Learning Path
PC Basics→Internet→Networks→Linux→Programming→Security→Web Attacks→Tools→Pentest

No favorites yet
Click the heart on any term page to add it here. Your favorites become your personal study list — the terms you want to revisit and master.

Cherry Root — Master Mind Map
├─ BLOCK 0 · PC Basics
│ ├─ Hardware: Computer, CPU, RAM, Storage
│ ├─ Software: OS, Software, Process

│ └─ Base: Binary, Bit/Byte, User, Boot
├─ BLOCK 1 · Internet
│ ├─ Identity: IP, DNS, URL

│ ├─ Roles: Browser, Server, API

│ └─ Risks: Email, Phishing
├─ BLOCK 2 · Networks
│ ├─ Basics: Network, LAN/WAN, OSI
│ ├─ Transport: TCP, UDP
│ ├─ Address: MAC, Ports, Subnet
│ ├─ Devices: Router, Switch, Firewall
│ ├─ Traffic: Packet, Bandwidth
│ └─ Services: Proxy, VPN, NAT, DHCP
├─ BLOCK 3 · Linux

│ ├─ Files: FS Tree, Permissions
│ ├─ Identity: Users, sudo

│ ├─ Risk: Threat, Vulnerability, Exploit, Risk, Attack Surface
│ └─ Controls: Encryption, Hashing, AuthN, AuthZ
├─ BLOCK 6 · Web Attacks
│ ├─ Injection: SQLi, XSS, Cmd Inj
│ ├─ Logic: CSRF, SSRF, Path Trav, IDOR
│ └─ Session: Open Redir, Clickjacking, Hijack, Auth Bypass, Race
├─ BLOCK 7 · Pentest Tools
│ ├─ Recon: nmap, Gobuster, Wireshark
│ ├─ Web: Burp, sqlmap, curl, DevTools
│ ├Exploit: Metasploit, netcat
│ └─ Crack: John, Hashcat, Hydra
├─ BLOCK 8 · Methodology
│ ├─ Discover: Recon, Scan, Enum
│ ├─ Breach: Exploit, PrivEsc, Persist, Lateral
│ └─ Close: Exfil, Cover Tracks, Report
├─ BLOCK 9 · Social Engineering
│ ├─ Digital: Phishing, Spear, Smishing

├─ BLOCK 10 · Malware

│ ├─ Stealth: Trojan, Rootkit, Backdoor

└─ BLOCK 11 · Defense
├─ Perimeter: Firewall, WAF, IDS/IPS
├─ Detect: SIEM, EDR, Honeypot
└─ Resilience: Patch Mgmt, Backup, IR, Awareness

cherry root › block 0 › B·01
Hardware
Everything you can physically touch on a computer.
B·01pc basicsphysical
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Everything you can physically touch on a computer."
Hardware is all the physical components — input devices (keyboard, mouse), output devices (monitor, speakers), storage (HDD, SSD), processing (CPU) and memory (RAM). Software cannot exist without hardware to run on.
② Explanation

hardware receiving it and ends with hardware displaying the result. The motherboard connects all components. In security, physical access is often the most dangerous — an attacker with physical access can bypass most software protections.

③ Keywords
hardwareinput deviceoutput devicestorageSSDHDDmotherboardperipheral
④ Where It Appears
Every physical device
Computers and laptops
Servers
Phones and IoT
⑤ How It Works
1Input device receives your action
2Signal travels through the motherboard
3CPU processes the instruction
4Result sent to output device
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Physical install of keyloggers
  • Stealing drives to extract data
Defense
  • Physical security controls
  • Full-disk encryption
  • Locked BIOS
⑦ Remember This
Software is what you see. Hardware is what makes it possible. No hardware = no computer.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·02
CPU
The brain of the computer — executes every single instruction.
B·02pc basicsprocessing
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The brain of the computer — it executes every instruction."
The CPU processes everything. Every calculation, every program, every action goes through it. It works directly with RAM to fetch and run instructions.
② Explanation

CPUs have cores — more cores means more things run simultaneously. The clock speed is in GHz — billions of cycles per second. In security, exploits like buffer overflows are designed around how the CPU handles memory. CPU-level bugs like Spectre can leak data across isolated processes.

③ Keywords
CPUprocessorcorex86x64ARMregistersclock speed
⑤ How It Works
1Fetches instruction from RAM
2Decodes the instruction
3Executes the operation
4Stores result back to RAM
5Repeats billions of times per second
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Spectre and Meltdown CPU data leaks
  • Buffer overflows target instruction flow
Defense
  • Keep firmware and OS updated
  • CPU patches applied at OS level
⑦ Remember This
The CPU reads instructions from memory. This is why memory exploits work — they put malicious instructions where the CPU will find them.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·03
RAM
Fast temporary memory — holds everything currently active.
B·03pc basicsmemory
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Fast temporary memory the computer uses while running programs."
RAM holds everything currently active. Wiped completely when you turn the computer off. Much faster than disk but nothing persists.
② Explanation

When you open a file it loads from disk into RAM so the CPU can access it quickly. RAM is split into the stack (function calls, local variables) and heap (dynamic data). In security, RAM can hold passwords and encryption keys — making RAM forensics a real attack and investigation technique.

③ Keywords
RAMvolatile memoryheapstackmemory dumpbufferRAM forensics
⑤ How It Works
1You open a program
2OS loads it from disk into RAM
3CPU reads and writes to RAM as it runs
4When closed, RAM space is freed
5On shutdown, all RAM is wiped
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • RAM dumps extract passwords from memory
  • Cold boot attacks recover RAM after shutdown
Defense
  • Full-disk encryption
  • Lock screen when away
⑦ Remember This
RAM = volatile (disappears on shutdown). Disk = persistent (stays). Critical difference in forensics.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·04
Software
Instructions that tell hardware what to do — no physical form.
B·04pc basicsprograms
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Instructions that tell hardware what to do."
Software is any program, app or script. It exists only as code and runs on hardware using the CPU and RAM. No physical form.
② Explanation

Divided into system software (OS), application software (browser, apps) and malicious software (malware). Every app and website runs as software. The difference between a program and malware is only intent — the mechanics are the same.

③ Keywords
softwareapplicationsource codeexecutablebinaryfirmware
⑤ How It Works
1Developer writes source code
2Compiled into binary the CPU understands
3OS loads the binary into RAM
4CPU executes line by line
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Malware disguises as legit software
  • Trojanized apps carry hidden payloads
Defense
  • Only install from trusted sources
  • Keep everything updated
⑦ Remember This
The line between good software and malware is intent. The mechanics are exactly the same.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·05
Operating System
The software that manages everything else on the computer.
B·05pc basicsOSLinux
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The software that manages everything else on the computer."
The OS sits between hardware and apps. Without it, hardware is just metal. It manages memory, runs programs, handles files, controls devices and manages users.
② Explanation

Windows, macOS and Linux are the most common. In cybersecurity, Linux is dominant — most tools, servers and targets run on it. The kernelhardware. Whoever controls the OS controls everything on the machine.

③ Keywords
OSkernelWindowsLinuxmacOSbootloaderBIOSUEFI
⑤ How It Works
1Power on — BIOS/UEFI starts
2Bootloader loads OS kernel into RAM
3Kernel takes control of hardware
4OS starts services and loads UI
5You interact with apps — OS manages underneath
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Exploit OS vulnerabilities for deep access
  • Unpatched OS = open door
Defense
  • Keep OS updated always
  • Remove unused services
⑦ Remember This
The OS is the most powerful software on any machine. Whoever controls the OS controls everything on it.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·06
Binary & Hex
The number systems computers actually think in.
B·06pc basicsencoding
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The number systems computers actually think in."
Binary = base-2, only 0s and 1s. Hex = base-16, uses 0–9 and A–F. Everything stored on a computer is ultimately binary. Hex is shorthand for binary that humans can read more easily.
② Explanation

Computers only understand electricity — on or off, 1 or 0. 8 bits make 1 byte. You will see hex constantly in security — packet captures, memory dumps, file headers, exploit code. The letter A is 01000001 in binary and 41 in hex.

③ Keywords
binaryhexbase-2base-16bitbyteASCIIencoding
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Shellcode written in hex
  • Hex-encoded input bypasses filters
Defense
  • Decode input before processing
  • Detect suspicious encoding patterns
⑦ Remember This
Hex 41 = Binary 01000001 = Letter A. Once this clicks, raw data stops being intimidating.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·07
File System
The system that organizes and addresses every file on disk.
B·07pc basicsstorage
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The system that gives every file an address so the OS can find it."
Without a file system, storage would be one giant unreadable blob. It creates folders, file names and addresses so data can be stored and retrieved reliably.
② Explanation

Windows uses NTFS, Linux uses ext4, USB drives use FAT32. Knowing the file system means knowing where logs live (/var/log), where passwords are stored (/etc/shadow) and where to look during an investigation. On Linux, everything is a file — even devices and processes.

③ Keywords
file systemNTFSext4FAT32directoryinodemount point
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Navigate to find credentials and configs
  • Hide malware in obscure directories
Defense
  • Strict permissions on sensitive files
  • Encrypt sensitive directories
⑦ Remember This
On Linux, everything is a file — even devices and processes. Master the file system and you master Linux.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·08
Path
The full address of a file inside the system.
B·08pc basicsnavigation
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The full address of a file inside the file system."
Absolute paths start from root. Relative paths start from your current location. The .. symbol means go up one level.
② Explanation

Absolute: /etc/passwd on Linux — always the same. Relative: ../config — depends where you are. In security, path traversal attacks use ../../ to escape allowed directories and reach sensitive files the attacker should not access.

③ Keywords
pathabsolute pathrelative path../path traversal
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Path traversal: ../../etc/passwd
  • Escape web root to read system files
Defense
  • Validate and sanitize all file paths
  • Never trust user-supplied paths
⑦ Remember This
Seeing ../ in a URL is a red flag. Someone might be trying to escape the intended directory.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·09
Permissions
Rules that define who can read, write or run a file.
B·09pc basicsaccess control
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Rules that define who can read, write or run a file."
Every file has permissions: read (r), write (w), execute (x) — applied to owner, group and everyone else.
② Explanation

On Linux: rwxr-xr-- means owner gets all, group can read and execute, others can only read. Change with chmod, ownership with chown. Misconfigured permissions are one of the most common privilege escalation vectors.

③ Keywords
permissionschmodchownrwxACLSUIDleast privilege
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • World-writable files for priv escalation
  • SUID binaries to run as root
Defense
  • Least privilege — minimum access only
  • Audit permissions regularly
⑦ Remember This
chmod 777 = everyone can do everything. Never use on anything sensitive.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·10
User & Admin
Regular users have limited power. Admins have full control.
B·10pc basicsprivilege
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Regular users have limited power. Admins have full control."
User levels define how much someone can do. Admin or root can do anything — install software, read all files, change all settings.
② Explanation

Standard users access only their own files. Root on Linux has zero restrictions. In hacking, the goal is often to start as a low-privilege user and escalate to root. On Linux, sudo borrows root power temporarily for one command.

③ Keywords
useradminrootprivilege escalationsudoUACleast privilege
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Privilege escalation from user to root
  • Exploit misconfigured sudo rules
Defense
  • Principle of least privilege
  • Nobody gets more access than needed
⑦ Remember This
Getting root = goal of most attacks. Protecting root = goal of all defense.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·11
Terminal / CLI
A text interface where you type commands to control the system.
B·11pc basicsshell
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A text interface where you type commands to control the system."
No icons, no clicking — just you and the machine. Every cybersecurity tool lives here. Non-negotiable skill.
② Explanation

The shell (usually bash) interprets your commands. You type, shell interprets, OS executes, result prints. The terminal connects directly to the OS — where the most powerful and dangerous actions happen. All major security tools run here.

③ Keywords
terminalCLIshellbashzshstdinstdoutstderr
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Run tools and payloads silently
  • Navigate and exfiltrate data
Defense
  • Log all terminal activity
  • Restrict shell access on servers
⑦ Remember This
The terminal feels scary at first. After a few weeks it feels like a superpower. Push through.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·12
Environment Variables
System-wide values stored in memory that any program can read.
B·12pc basicssystem
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"System-wide values stored in memory that programs can read."
Like global settings available to any running program. Set by the OS or user, readable by any process in that session.
② Explanation

PATH = where programs are installed. USER = current user. HOME = home folder. Run envterminal to see all. In security, API keys and passwords accidentally stored in ENV are a very common misconfiguration found in real production systems.

③ Keywords
env variablesPATHHOMEUSERexportenv$VAR
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • API keys in ENV leak to other processes
  • PATH manipulation runs malicious programs
Defense
  • Never store secrets in env variables
  • Use dedicated secret managers
⑦ Remember This
If an attacker controls PATH, they control what runs when you type any command.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·13
Process
A program that is currently running — each with its own ID and memory.
B·13pc basicsexecution
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A program that is currently running."
A process is software actively executed by the CPU. Each has a unique PID and its own memory space.
② Explanation

process with its own PID. Processes can spawn threads — smaller execution units within the same process. In security, malware runs as a process. Process injection embeds malicious code inside a legitimate process to evade detection.

③ Keywords
processPIDps auxtopthreadprocess injection
⑤ How It Works
1You open a program
2OS loads it into RAM and assigns PID
3CPU executes its instructions
4Process ends when finished or closed
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Malware hides as a process
  • Process injection in legit processes
Defense
  • Monitor running processes
  • Investigate anything unfamiliar
⑦ Remember This
ps aux shows every running process. Learn to read it — first thing to check when something feels wrong.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·14
Port (local)
A numbered channel a program uses to send or receive data.
B·14pc basicsnetworking
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A numbered channel a program uses to send or receive data."
Ports let one machine run many services simultaneously. IP address = building, port = specific door number.
② Explanation

Port 80 = HTTP, 443 = HTTPS, 22 = SSH, 21 = FTP. There are 65,535 possible ports. Scanning for open ports is the first step of almost every attack — it reveals what services are running and what might be exploitable.

③ Keywords
portopen portlisteningport 22port 80port 443port scanning
⑤ How It Works
1A service starts and listens on a port
2Client connects to IP + port number
3Data flows through that channel
4Multiple services run on different ports at once
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Port scan to find exposed services
  • Exploit services on open ports
Defense
  • Close all unused ports
  • Firewall to restrict external access
⑦ Remember This
Nmap's entire job is finding open ports. If it's open, it's a potential door in.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 0 › B·QR
Quick Reference
Block 0 at a glance — fast lookup for key concepts.
Key Ports
port 22
SSHremote access
port 23
Telnetunencrypted — avoid
port 21
FTP
port 53
DNS
port 80
HTTPunencrypted web
port 443
HTTPSencrypted web
port 3306
MySQL
port 3389
RDPWindows remote
Binary to Hex to Text
01000001
hex: 41text: A
01000010
hex: 42text: B
1 bit= one 0 or 1
8 bits= 1 byte
Linux Permissions
rwxr-xr--owner: all · group: r+x · others: r only
chmod 777everyone can do everything — dangerous!
chmod 600owner read and write only — secure for keys
sudo [cmd]run as root temporarily
Process and System Commands
ps auxlist all running processes
toplive process monitor
kill [PID]end a process by its ID
whoamishow current user
envshow all environment variables
Path Symbols
/root directory (Linux)
~home folder
.current folder
..one level up
../path traversal pattern — red flag in URLs
cherry root › block 0 › B·MM
Mind Map
Block 0 — quick visual summaries of every term in order.
B·01 — Hardware
Tudo que você pode tocar. Teclado, mouse, monitor, HD/SSD, CPU, RAM.
Você age → hardware recebe → CPU processa → resultado aparece
Hardware = parte física. Sem ele, nada existe.
B·02 — CPU
O cérebro. Executa toda instrução de todo programa.
Busca instrução na RAM → decodifica → executa → salva → repete bilhões de vezes/segundo
CPU = quem faz o trabalho. Tudo passa por ela.
B·03 — RAM
Memória temporária e rápida. Guarda tudo que está ativo agora.
Abre programa → OS carrega na RAM → CPU usa → fecha → memória liberada → desliga → tudo some
RAM = curto prazo. Rápida mas temporária.
B·04 — Software
Instruções que dizem ao hardware o que fazer. Sem forma física.
Código escrito → compilado em binário → OS carrega na RAM → CPU executa
A diferença entre software e malware é só a intenção.
B·05 — Operating System
Gerencia tudo. Liga hardware e apps.
Liga o PC → BIOS → bootloader → kernel → OS assume → você interage
Quem controla o OS controla tudo.
B·06 — Binary and Hex
Os sistemas numéricos reais do computador.
01000001 → 41 → A (tudo é armazenado assim)
Hex é só binário mais legível. Vai aparecer em todo lugar.
B·07 — File System
Organiza e endereça arquivos no disco. Windows = NTFS · Linux = ext4.
OS recebe pedido → busca índice → acha localização → lê → carrega na RAM
No Linux, tudo é um arquivo — até dispositivos e processos.
B·08 — Path
Endereço completo de um arquivo. Absoluto ou relativo.
/ = raiz · ~ = home · . = aqui · .. = um nível acima · ../ = path traversal
../ em uma URL = sinal de alerta.
B·09 — Permissions
Quem pode ler, escrever ou executar cada arquivo.
rwxr-xr-- → Owner: tudo · Group: r+x · Others: só lê
chmod 777 = todo mundo pode tudo. Nunca em arquivos sensíveis.
B·10 — User and Admin
Usuários têm acesso limitado. Admin/root tem controle total.
Login → OS verifica nível → ação normal ou pede elevação → sudo = root temporário
Conseguir root = objetivo de todo ataque. Proteger root = toda defesa.
B·11 — Terminal / CLI
Interface de texto onde você controla o sistema digitando comandos.
Você digita → shell interpreta → OS executa → resultado aparece
Toda ferramenta de segurança vive aqui. Aprenda e tudo abre.
B·12 — Environment Variables
Valores globais que qualquer programa pode ler. PATH, USER, HOME…
OS define → programas leem → echo $PATH mostra onde buscar programas
Segredos em env variables vazam fácil. Nunca guarde senhas aí.
B·13 — Process
Um programa em execução com seu próprio PID e espaço de memória.
Você abre → OS carrega na RAM → PID atribuído → CPU executa → encerra quando termina
Malware é um processo. ps aux mostra tudo que roda agora.
B·14 — Port (local)
Canal numerado por onde um serviço envia e recebe dados.
Serviço abre e escuta → cliente conecta no IP + porta → dados fluem por esse canal
Porta aberta = porta de entrada. Feche o que não usa.
cherry root › block 0 › B·MN
My Notes
Free space — write discoveries, questions and connections here.
cherry root › block 1 › W·01
Internet vs Web
Not the same thing — and understanding why matters.
W·01internetfundamentals
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The internet is the infrastructure. The web is one service on top of it."
The internet is the global network of interconnected computers. The web (World Wide Web) is just one of many services that run on it — the one you access through a browser using HTTP.
② Explanation

Think of the internet as the road system and the web as one type of vehicle that uses those roads. Email, SSH, FTP, VoIP, gaming — all these also use the internet but are not the web.

The web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989. It uses HTTP/HTTPS to transfer pages, HTML to structure content and URLs to address resources. When you type a website address in a browser, you are using the web — but the connection you make travels over the internet.

③ Keywords
internetwebWorld Wide WebHTTPbrowsernetworkprotocolHTML
④ Where It Appears
Every website you visit
Browser address bar
Security recon
Network architecture
⑦ Remember This
Internet = the roads. Web = one type of car. Email, SSH and gaming also use the internet but are NOT the web.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·02
IP Address
The unique numerical address of a device on a network.
W·02internetnetworking
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The address of a device on a network — like a home address for computers."
Every device connected to a network has an IP address. It is used to identify who is sending data and where to send the response.
② Explanation

There are two versions. IPv4 uses 4 numbers separated by dots: 192.168.0.1. There are about 4 billion possible IPv4 addresses, which are running out. IPv6 is the newer version with a much larger address space: 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334.

Private IPs (like 192.168.x.x) are used inside your home or office network. Public IPs are the ones visible on the internet. NAT translates between them. In security, your IP address can reveal your approximate location and ISP — making IP tracking and VPNs important topics.

③ Keywords
IP addressIPv4IPv6private IPpublic IPNATsubnetlocalhost
④ Where It Appears
Every network connection
Server logs
Recon and scanning
Firewall rules
⑤ How It Works
1Your device requests an IP from the router via DHCP
2Router assigns a private IP (e.g. 192.168.1.5)
3When you go online, your ISP provides a public IP
4NAT translates between your private and public IP
5Other servers see your public IP, not your private one
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • IP scanning to find live targets
  • IP spoofing to fake the source address
  • Geolocation via IP to profile targets
Defense
  • Use a VPN to hide your public IP
  • Firewall rules to block suspicious IPs
  • Rate limiting and IP blocking
⑦ Remember This
Your public IP is visible to every website you visit. It can reveal your location. A VPN masks it by replacing your real IP with the VPN server's IP.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·03
DNS
The internet's phonebook — translates domain names into IP addresses.
W·03internetprotocol
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The system that translates domain names into IP addresses."
When you type google.com, your computer does not know what that means — it needs an IP address. DNS is the system that looks up that translation automatically.
② Explanation

DNS works in a hierarchy. First your computer checks its local cache. Then it asks a DNS resolver (usually your ISP or a public one like Google's 8.8.8.8). The resolver works up the chain — root servers, TLD servers (.com, .org), and finally the authoritative nameserver for that domain.

DNS records come in types: A record = domain to IPv4, AAAA = domain to IPv6, MX = mail server, CNAME = alias, TXT = verification and other text. DNS is critical in recon — querying DNS records reveals infrastructure details about a target.

③ Keywords
DNSdomain nameDNS resolverA recordMX recordCNAMETXT recordTTLnameserver
⑤ How It Works
1You type google.com in the browser
2Computer checks local DNS cache
3If not cached, asks DNS resolver (e.g. 8.8.8.8)
4Resolver asks root server → TLD server → authoritative server
5IP is returned and browser connects to it
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • DNS spoofing — inject fake DNS responses
  • DNS cache poisoning — corrupt resolver cache
  • Subdomain takeover — claim abandoned subdomains
  • DNS tunneling — hide data in DNS queries
Defense
  • Use DNSSEC to authenticate records
  • Use trusted resolvers (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8)
  • Audit and remove unused subdomains
⑦ Remember This
DNS is often the first thing a hacker probes. Tools like nslookup, dig and dnsx can reveal a lot about a target just from its DNS records.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·04
HTTP / HTTPS
The language browsers and servers use to exchange data.
W·04internetprotocolweb
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The protocol that loads every web page you have ever seen."
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the set of rules browsers and servers follow to request and send web content. HTTPS is the same thing but with encryption — so no one in the middle can read what is being sent.
② Explanation

When you visit a website, your browser sends an HTTP request to the server. The server responds with an HTTP response containing the content and a status code (200 = OK, 404 = not found, 500 = server error).

HTTP is stateless — it does not remember you between requests. That is why cookies and sessions exist. HTTPS wraps HTTP inside TLS encryption — even if someone intercepts the traffic, they only see gibberish. The S in HTTPS stands for Secure and in security this difference is everything.

HTTP methods you will see constantly: GET (fetch data), POST (send data), PUT (update), DELETE (remove), OPTIONS (check what methods are supported).

③ Keywords
HTTPHTTPSrequestresponseGETPOSTPUTDELETETLSstatelessport 80port 443
④ Where It Appears
Every website visit
Burp Suite intercepts
API calls
Web attack testing
⑤ How It Works
1Browser sends HTTP request: GET /page HTTP/1.1
2Request includes headers (Host, User-Agent, cookies…)
3Server processes and sends back HTTP response
4Response includes status code + headers + body (the page)
5Browser renders the content
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • MITM on HTTP — intercept unencrypted traffic
  • SSL stripping — downgrade HTTPS to HTTP
  • HTTP header injection
Defense
  • Always use HTTPS — never send sensitive data over HTTP
  • Enable HSTS (forces HTTPS on browsers)
  • Use secure and HttpOnly cookie flags
⑦ Remember This
HTTP requests and responses are the heartbeat of the web. Once you understand them deeply — methods, headers, status codes — you will start to see vulnerabilities everywhere. Tools like Burp Suite live in this layer.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·05
URL
The full address of any resource on the web.
W·05internetweb
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The complete address that tells a browser exactly what to fetch and from where."
URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator. Every page, image, file and API endpoint on the web has a unique URL.
② Explanation

A URL has several parts: https://example.com:443/page?id=1#section

https = protocol / example.com = domain / :443 = port (optional, default for HTTPS) / /page = path / ?id=1 = query string / #section = fragment (anchor)

In security, URLs are attack vectors. The query string (?id=1IDOR and path traversal attacks start. Attackers also use URLs for phishing — making fake domains that look like real ones.

③ Keywords
URLdomainpathquery stringparameterfragmentprotocolsubdomain
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Inject payloads in query parameters
  • Path traversal via URL path
  • Phishing with look-alike domains
Defense
  • Validate and sanitize all URL parameters
  • Use allowlists for expected values
⑦ Remember This
The query string in a URL is where most web attacks begin. Always treat URL parameters as untrusted user input.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·06
Request & Response
How the browser and server talk to each other.
W·06internetHTTP
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The browser asks (request). The server answers (response)."
Every interaction on the web follows this cycle. The browser sends a request with what it wants, and the server sends back a response with the content and a status code.
② Explanation

An HTTP request has: a method (GET, POST…), a path (/login), headers (metadata like cookies, User-Agent, Content-Type) and optionally a body (data being sent).

An HTTP responsestatus code (200, 404, 500…), headers (metadata like Content-Type, Set-Cookie) and a body (the HTML, JSON or file being returned).

In security, intercepting and modifying requests is the foundation of web testing. Tools like Burp Suite sit between the browser and server and let you read, replay and modify every request and response.

③ Keywords
requestresponseHTTP methodheadersbodystatus codeBurp Suiteintercept
⑤ How It Works
1Browser builds a request (method + path + headers + body)
2Request is sent over TCP to the server's IP and port
3Server receives and processes the request
4Server sends back a response (status + headers + body)
5Browser renders the response content
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Intercept and modify requests to change parameters
  • Replay requests to repeat actions
  • Fuzz requests to find unexpected behavior
Defense
  • Validate all request data server-side
  • Use CSRF tokens to prevent replayed requests
⑦ Remember This
Everything a web app does is a request and response. If you can intercept and modify them, you can test for almost every web vulnerability.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·07
Status Codes
The server's numeric reply telling you what happened with your request.
W·07internetHTTP
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Three-digit codes the server sends to tell you if your request worked."
Every HTTP response includes a status code. They are grouped by their first digit: 1xx = informational, 2xx = success, 3xx = redirection, 4xx = client error, 5xx = server error.
② Explanation

The most important ones to know: 200 OK = success. 201 Created = resource created. 301/302 = redirect (permanent/temporary). 400 Bad Request = malformed request. 401 Unauthorized = not authenticated. 403 Forbidden = authenticated but not allowed. 404 Not Found = resource does not exist. 500 Internal Server Error = something broke on the server.

In security, status codes reveal a lot. A 403 means the resource exists but is blocked. A 200broken auth. A 500 might indicate an injection worked.

③ Keywords
status code200 OK301 redirect401 unauthorized403 forbidden404 not found500 server error
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • 403 reveals resource exists — try to bypass
  • 500 on injection input = possible vulnerability
  • Different codes on valid vs invalid users = enumeration
Defense
  • Return generic error messages
  • Do not reveal internal details in 500 errors
⑦ Remember This
Status codes are clues. A hacker reads them like a map — each code tells you something about what is on the server and how it behaves.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·08
Headers
Metadata sent alongside every HTTP request and response.
W·08internetHTTP
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Extra information attached to every HTTP request and response."
Headers are key-value pairs that carry metadata — things like what type of content is being sent, who is sending it, what cookies exist and how the response should be cached.
② Explanation

Common request headers: Host (which domain you want), User-Agent (what browser you are using), Cookiesession data), Content-Type (format of the body), Authorization (your auth token).

Common response headers: Content-Type (format of the response), Set-Cookie (server setting a cookie), Location (redirect destination), X-Frame-Options (prevent clickjacking), Content-Security-Policy (XSS protection rules).

In security, headers leak information and can be manipulated. Missing security headers are a common finding in pentests and bug bounties.

③ Keywords
headersHostUser-AgentCookieAuthorizationContent-TypeSet-CookieCSPHSTS
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Host header injection to manipulate routing
  • Missing security headers enable XSS or clickjacking
  • Leak server info via Server and X-Powered-By headers
Defense
  • Add security headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options
  • Remove headers that leak server info
  • Validate Host header on the server side
⑦ Remember This
Security headers are free protections. Missing CSP, HSTS or X-Frame-Options is a finding on almost every pentest. Always check what headers a server returns.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·09
Cookies
Small files stored in the browser to remember user state between requests.
W·09internetsessions
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Small pieces of data the server stores in your browser to remember you."
HTTP is stateless — it forgets you after every request. Cookies solve this by storing a small value in the browser that gets sent with every future request to that site.
② Explanation

When you log in, the server creates a session and sends back a cookie containing your session ID. Your browser stores it and sends it with every subsequent request — that is how the server knows who you are.

Cookie attributes matter in security: HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie (blocks XSS cookie theft). SecureHTTPS. SameSite protects against CSRF. Missing these flags are very common vulnerabilities.

③ Keywords
cookiesession IDHttpOnlySecure flagSameSiteSet-Cookiecookie theftsession hijacking
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • XSS to steal session cookie = account takeover
  • Session fixation — force victim to use known session ID
  • Session hijacking via intercepted cookie
Defense
  • Set HttpOnly flag — JavaScript cannot read cookie
  • Set Secure flag — only sent over HTTPS
  • Set SameSite=Strict to prevent CSRF
⑦ Remember This
Stealing a session cookie = stealing someone's logged-in session. This is why XSS is so dangerous — it can silently extract cookies and hand them to an attacker.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·10
Sessions
The server-side mechanism that keeps track of a logged-in user.
W·10internetauthentication
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A temporary record the server keeps to remember who you are."
When you log in, the server creates a session — a stored record on the server side — and gives you a session ID via a cookie. Every request you make, the server checks that ID to know who you are.
② Explanation

Sessions have a lifecycle: they are created at login, used with every request and destroyedsession ID in your cookie is the key — whoever has it can impersonate you.

Session management vulnerabilities are in the OWASP Top 10. Common issues: session IDs that do not expire, predictable session IDs, sessions not invalidated after logout, and session fixation attacks.

③ Keywords
sessionsession IDsession hijackingsession fixationsession expirylogoutstateful
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Session hijacking — steal or predict a session ID
  • Session fixation — set a known session ID before login
  • Sessions that never expire stay usable forever
Defense
  • Regenerate session ID after login
  • Set session expiry and inactivity timeouts
  • Invalidate session completely on logout
⑦ Remember This
The session ID is the crown jewel. Protect it like a password — because it is effectively the same thing.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·11
API
An interface that lets programs communicate with each other.
W·11internetweb
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A set of rules that lets two programs talk to each other."
API stands for Application Programming Interface. It defines how a program can request data or actions from another program — usually over HTTP using JSON.
② Explanation

When you use an app that shows the weather, it is probably calling a weather API. The app sends a request to the API endpoint (a URL), the API returns data in JSON format, and the app displays it.

REST APIsIDOR), verbose error messages and no rate limiting.

③ Keywords
APIRESTendpointJSONAPI keyrate limitingauthenticationIDOR
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Access unauthenticated API endpoints
  • IDOR through API object references
  • Brute force without rate limiting
  • Exposed API keys in source code or URLs
Defense
  • Authenticate every API endpoint
  • Implement rate limiting and throttling
  • Never expose API keys in client-side code
⑦ Remember This
APIs are the new attack surface. Many apps expose more data through their API than through the visible web interface. Always test the API when testing a web application.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·12
Client & Server
Who asks, who answers — the fundamental model of the web.
W·12internetarchitecture
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Client = who asks. Server = who answers."
In every web interaction, a client makes a request and a server responds. Your browser is the client. The computer hosting the website is the server.
② Explanation

The client initiates the communication — it sends requests and renders the responses. The server listens for incoming requests and sends back appropriate responses. A single server can handle thousands of clients simultaneously.

In security, this distinction matters because: client-side code (HTML, JavaScript, CSS) can always be viewed and modified by the user — never trust it. Server-side code runs on the server and is not visible to users — it is where real validation must happen. Never trust client-side input validation.

③ Keywords
clientserverclient-sideserver-sidebrowserweb serverrequestresponse
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Bypass client-side validation — it is trivially removable
  • Manipulate JavaScript to remove restrictions
  • Send requests the UI was not designed to allow
Defense
  • Always validate input on the server side
  • Never rely on client-side checks for security
⑦ Remember This
Anything the client sends can be faked. Real security only exists on the server. Client-side validation is for user experience, not security.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·13
CDN
Distributed servers around the world that speed up content delivery.
W·13internetinfrastructure
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A network of servers that deliver content from the location closest to the user."
CDN stands for Content Delivery Network. Instead of everyone connecting to one server in one country, a CDN caches content on many servers worldwide — reducing load times and improving availability.
② Explanation

When you load a website using a CDN, the images, scripts and styles are served from a server geographically close to you — not necessarily the origin server. Popular CDNs include Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront and Akamai.

In security, CDNs can also act as a Firewall), filtering malicious traffic before it reaches the origin server. However, CDNs also introduce complexity — misconfigured CDNs can leak the origin server's real IP address, bypassing all protections.

③ Keywords
CDNContent Delivery NetworkCloudflarecachingedge serverorigin serverWAF
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Find origin server IP to bypass CDN protections
  • Cache poisoning — inject malicious content into cache
Defense
  • Never expose origin server IP in DNS or headers
  • Use CDN WAF rules to filter malicious traffic
⑦ Remember This
If the target uses a CDN, finding the real origin server IP is a key recon step. Once you bypass the CDN, you bypass the WAF protecting the app.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·14
SSL / TLS
The encryption layer that makes HTTPS secure.
W·14internetencryptionHTTPS
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The technology that encrypts data traveling between your browser and a server."
SSL (now replaced by TLS) is the cryptographic protocol that makes HTTPS work. It ensures the connection is encrypted and the server is who it claims to be.
② Explanation

SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) is the older name — it is now deprecated and insecure. TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the current standard. When people say SSL they usually mean TLS.

TLS provides three things: encryption (your data cannot be read in transit), authenticationHTTPScertificate) and integrity (data cannot be tampered with in transit). The TLS handshake happens before any HTTP data is exchanged — it establishes the encrypted tunnel first.

In security, outdated TLS versions (SSL 2/3, TLS 1.0/1.1) are vulnerable. Tools like Nmap and testssl.sh can check which versions a server supports.

③ Keywords
SSLTLSHTTPScertificatehandshakeencryptionCAcipher suitePKI
⑤ How It Works
1Client connects to server and sends supported TLS versions
2Server responds with its certificate and chosen cipher
3Client verifies the certificate (is it valid? issued by trusted CA?)
4They exchange encryption keys using asymmetric crypto
5Encrypted tunnel established — HTTP data flows inside it
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • SSL stripping — downgrade HTTPS to HTTP
  • Weak cipher suites — crack older encryption
  • Invalid or self-signed certificates can be spoofed
Defense
  • Use TLS 1.2 or 1.3 minimum
  • Enable HSTS to prevent SSL stripping
  • Use certificates from trusted Certificate Authorities
⑦ Remember This
HTTPS without valid TLS is not secure. The padlock in the browser just means the connection is encrypted — it does not mean the site is safe or legitimate.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 1 › W·QR
Quick Reference
Block 1 at a glance — status codes, headers and URL anatomy.
HTTP Status Codes
200 OKrequest succeeded
201 Createdresource created successfully
301 / 302redirect — permanent / temporary
400Bad Request — malformed or invalid
401Unauthorized — not authenticated
403Forbidden — authenticated but not allowed
404Not Found — resource does not exist
429Too Many Requests — rate limited
500Internal Server Error — server broke
503Service Unavailable — server down
URL Anatomy
https://protocol
example.comdomain
:443port (optional)
/page/pathresource path
?id=1&q=testquery string — attack surface!
#sectionfragment — client-side only
HTTP Methods
GETfetch a resource — no body
POSTsend data to create or process
PUTupdate a resource
DELETEremove a resource
OPTIONSask what methods are supported
Key Security Headers
CSPContent-Security-Policy — controls what can run on the page
HSTSStrict-Transport-Security — forces HTTPS
X-Frame-Optionsprevents clickjacking
HttpOnlycookie flag — JS cannot read the cookie
Securecookie flag — only sent over HTTPS
SameSitecookie flag — prevents CSRF
DNS Record Types
A recorddomain to IPv4 address
AAAA recorddomain to IPv6 address
MX recordmail server for the domain
CNAMEalias — points to another domain
TXT recordtext data — used for verification, SPF
cherry root › block 1 › W·MM
Mind Map
Block 1 — quick visual summaries of every term in order.
W·01 — Internet vs Web
Internet = a rede global de computadores. Web = um serviço que roda em cima dela usando HTTP.
Internet = estradas. Web = um tipo de carro. Email e SSH também usam as estradas.
Não é a mesma coisa. A web é só uma parte da internet.
W·02 — IP Address
O endereço único de um dispositivo na rede. IPv4 = 4 números. IPv6 = mais longo.
Dispositivo liga → DHCP atribui IP privado → NAT traduz pra IP público → internet enxerga o IP público
Seu IP público é visível pra todo site que você visita. VPN mascara isso.
W·03 — DNS
Traduz nomes de domínio em IPs. A agenda telefônica da internet.
Você digita google.com → cache local → DNS resolver → root → TLD → authoritative → retorna o IP
DNS é o primeiro lugar que um hacker investiga. Revela muito sobre a infraestrutura.
W·04 — HTTP / HTTPS
O protocolo que carrega páginas web. HTTP = não criptografado. HTTPS = criptografado com TLS.
Browser envia request → servidor processa → envia response com status code e conteúdo
HTTP é a camada onde quase todos os ataques web acontecem. Aprenda bem.
W·05 — URL
O endereço completo de um recurso web. Protocolo + domínio + caminho + query string.
https://site.com/page?id=1 → protocol / domain / path / query string
O query string é onde a maioria dos ataques começa. Sempre trate parâmetros como input não confiável.
W·06 — Request and Response
Browser pergunta (request). Servidor responde (response). Cada interação na web segue esse ciclo.
Request: method + path + headers + body → Response: status code + headers + body
Se você consegue interceptar e modificar requests, consegue testar quase toda vulnerabilidade web.
W·07 — Status Codes
O código numérico que o servidor retorna dizendo o que aconteceu com o request.
2xx = request succeeded · 3xx = redirect · 4xx = erro do cliente · 5xx = erro do servidor
Status codes são pistas. 403 = recurso existe mas bloqueado. 500 em injection = possível vuln.
W·08 — Headers
Metadados enviados em todo request e response. Carregam cookies, tipos de conteúdo, regras de segurança.
Request headers: Host, User-Agent, Cookie · Response headers: Set-Cookie, CSP, HSTS
Headers de segurança faltando são um achado em quase todo pentest. Sempre cheque.
W·09 — Cookies
Pequenos dados que o servidor armazena no browser para lembrar o usuário entre requests.
Login → servidor cria sessão → envia cookie com session ID → browser manda em todo request futuro
Roubar o session cookie = roubar a sessão logada. É por isso que XSS é tão perigoso.
W·10 — Sessions
Registro server-side que mantém o estado do usuário logado. O session ID no cookie é a chave.
Login → session criada no servidor → ID enviado via cookie → cada request verifica o ID
O session ID é a coroa. Proteja como uma senha — porque é exatamente isso.
W·11 — API
Interface que deixa programas se comunicarem. REST APIs usam HTTP e retornam JSON.
App envia GET /weather → API retorna JSON com dados → app exibe pro usuário
APIs são a nova superfície de ataque. Sempre teste a API quando testar uma aplicação web.
W·12 — Client and Server
Cliente = quem pede. Servidor = quem responde. Código client-side pode sempre ser modificado.
Browser (client) → envia request → servidor processa → envia response → browser renderiza
Nunca confie em validação client-side. Segurança real só existe no servidor.
W·13 — CDN
Rede de servidores distribuídos que entrega conteúdo do ponto mais próximo do usuário.
Usuário requisita → CDN verifica cache → serve do edge server mais próximo ou busca do origin
Encontrar o IP do servidor de origem bypassa o CDN e a WAF que o protege.
W·14 — SSL / TLS
O protocolo criptográfico que faz o HTTPS funcionar. Garante encriptação, autenticação e integridade.
Client conecta → handshake → troca de chaves → túnel criptografado estabelecido → HTTP flui dentro
O cadeado no browser só significa que a conexão é criptografada — não que o site é seguro.
cherry root › block 1 › W·MN
My Notes
Free space — write discoveries, questions and connections here.
2
Block 2 · Networks
The Network Layer
Networks are how every attack travels. Before you can scan, intercept, or pivot, you need to understand how packets move, how routers decide paths, and why MAC differs from IP. This block builds the mental model every red and blue teamer needs.
TCP/UDPOSI ModelRouters FirewallsPortsVPNDHCP
17 terms · N·01 → N·17 how data travels
cherry root › block 2 › N·01
Network
The fundamental concept that everything else builds on.
N·01networkfundamentals
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Two or more devices that can talk to each other form a network."
A network is any group of connected devices that share data. Two laptops on the same WiFi form a network. The internet is a network of networks.
② Explanation

Every device on a network has an identity (an IP address) and uses agreed-upon rules (protocols) to communicate. Without a network, computers are isolated islands. With one, they can share files, services, and attacks.

Networks come in different sizes: a single home WiFi (LAN), a corporate network spanning buildings (WAN), or the entire internet. The principles of how they work are the same — only the scale changes.

③ Keywords
networkdeviceconnectioncommunicationLANWANtopology
④ Where It Appears
Every connected device — phone, laptop, IoT
Office and home WiFi
Cloud services and data centers
Every cyberattack starts on a network
⑤ How It Works
Devices connect via wired (Ethernet) or wireless (WiFi, cellular) links. Each device gets an IP address. When data needs to move, it gets broken into packets, each labeled with destination info. Routers and switches forward packets along the path. The receiving device reassembles them.
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Sniffing — capturing unencrypted traffic on the same network
  • Lateral movement — using one compromised device to reach others
  • Network mapping with tools like Nmap to find all alive hosts
Defense
