Quick wins: filter Network by Fetch/XHR for APIs. Right-click a request → Copy → cURL/fetch to reproduce. Application → Storage shows cookies — flip values to test access controls. Sources → Pretty print ({}) makes minified JS readable.

Hidden gems: command palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) jumps anywhere. monitorEvents(document, 'click') in console logs every click. Override response with Network → Override. Throttle to simulate slow networks.

③ Keywords
F12NetworkApplicationSourceslocalStoragecopy as fetchbreakpointCSP report
④ Where It Appears
Every web pentest reconnaissance step
SPA / heavy-JS app analysis
Cookie + storage inspection
API discovery from front-end
⑤ How It Works
1Open DevTools (F12)
2Reload with Network open to catch all calls
3Inspect cookies + storage
4Read JS in Sources for endpoints / secrets
5Use Console to call functions directly
⑥ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • Find API endpoints from minified JS
  • Edit DOM to bypass client-side checks
  • Tamper localStorage / cookies
  • Read JS source for hardcoded secrets
Detection
  • Never trust client-side validation
  • Don't ship secrets in frontend bundles
  • Set HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies
  • Source maps off in production
⑦ Remember This
If it runs in the browser, you can see it, edit it, replay it. F12 is the cheat code.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
8
Block 8 · Pentest Methodology
The Phases
A pentest isn't ad-hoc clicking — it's a phased methodology. Recon, exploit, escalate, pivot, persist, exfil, cover, report. Each phase maps to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and has its own techniques and tooling.
reconscanningexploitprivescpersistencelateralexfilreport
10 terms · M01 → M10 method beats luck
cherry root › block 8 › M·01
Reconnaissance
Passive and active gathering of target intelligence.
M·01reconintelpassive
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"You can't hit what you can't see. Recon is seeing."
Reconnaissance is phase 1 — collecting public and semi-public information about the target before sending a single packet that could trigger an alert. Splits into passive (no contact) and active (touches target).
② Explanation

Passive sources: OSINT — Google dorks, LinkedIn, GitHub, Shodan, Censys, crt.sh for certificates, DNS history, WHOIS, archive.org, breach databases (HaveIBeenPwned).

Active recon touches the target: port scans, banner grabs, web crawls, directory brute force. Loud but precise — you start measuring what's actually exposed today, not what was true last year.

MITRE ATT&CK tactic: TA0043 Reconnaissance. Techniques include T1595 Active Scanning, T1589 Gather Victim Identity, T1591 Gather Victim Org Info.

③ Keywords
OSINTShodanGoogle dorkscrt.shsubdomainsWHOISATT&CKT1595
④ Where It Appears
First phase of every engagement
Bug bounty asset discovery
Red team pre-engagement
Threat intel gathering
⑤ How It Works
1Define scope clearly
2Passive: gather OSINT
3Map domains, subdomains, IPs
4Active: scan + enumerate live assets
5Build target inventory
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Shodan + Censys for exposed services
  • Subdomain enum (amass, subfinder)
  • crt.sh for certificate transparency
  • Email harvest from LinkedIn
Defender side
  • Minimize public footprint
  • Domain monitoring for typo-squats
  • Hide internal IPs in DNS
  • Train staff against LinkedIn over-share
⑦ Remember This
The thinnest layer of public information is often the entire attack plan.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·02
Scanning
Port, service and version discovery on live targets.
M·02scanningportsservices
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Recon tells you who. Scanning tells you what's running."
Scanning probes live hosts for open ports, running services and software versions. The output is the menu — every service is a potential entry point with its own CVE history.
② Explanation

Layers: host discovery (which IPs answer?), port scan (which TCP/UDP ports are open?), service detection (what's listening?), version detection (which exact build?), vuln scan (which CVEs match?).

Tools: nmap is the standard. masscan for huge ranges (millions of IPs / minute). rustscan wraps masscan + nmap. Vuln scanners: Nessus, OpenVAS, Nuclei (template-based, fast).

MITRE: T1046 Network Service Scanning. Defender side appears in IDS as port-scan signatures, SYN flood patterns, distributed slow scans.

③ Keywords
nmapmasscanNucleiNessusCVEbannerversiontop-ports
④ Where It Appears
Right after recon
Continuous external attack-surface scanning
Compliance scans
Pre-exploitation
⑤ How It Works
1Confirm scope alignment
2Host discovery (ICMP / TCP ping)
3Port scan (top-1000 then full)
4Service + version detection
5Map findings to known CVEs
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • nmap -sS -sV -p- target
  • masscan for /16 ranges
  • nuclei -l urls.txt
  • Cross-ref versions with NVD
Defender side
  • IDS port-scan signatures
  • Minimize attack surface (close ports)
  • Patch services to non-vuln versions
  • Honeypots in scan range
⑦ Remember This
Every open port is a promise to talk to strangers. Close what doesn't need to listen.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·03
Enumeration
Extracting usernames, shares, banners and structural info.
M·03enumbannersusers
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Scanning finds the door. Enumeration reads the names on the buzzers."
Enumeration is targeted, deeper probing of each discovered service to pull structural information: usernames, group names, shares, mounts, software versions, configuration details.
② Explanation

Per-service tools: enum4linux / smbclient for SMB, ldapsearch for LDAP/AD, snmpwalk for SNMP, kerbrute for AD usernames, gobuster vhost for HTTP vhosts.

AD enumeration is huge: BloodHound visualizes attack paths from any user to Domain Admin. PowerView / SharpHound collect the data. Critical pre-exploit step on internal pentests.

MITRE: T1087 Account Discovery, T1135 Network Share Discovery, T1018 Remote System Discovery.

③ Keywords
enum4linuxBloodHoundLDAPSMBSNMPbannerskerbrutevhost
④ Where It Appears
Internal AD environments
Post-recon detail collection
Pre-exploitation prep
Compliance asset inventory
⑤ How It Works
1Pick service from scan output
2Run service-specific enum tool
3Collect names, shares, configs
4Cross-reference with previous data
5Identify exploit paths
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • enum4linux -a target
  • BloodHound graph queries
  • kerbrute userenum users.txt
  • SNMP community string brute
Defender side
  • Disable null sessions
  • Restrict LDAP/SMB anonymous binds
  • Change default SNMP communities
  • Tier admin accounts (no shared)
⑦ Remember This
Enumeration is the difference between knowing a service exists and knowing how it's used.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·04
Exploitation
Triggering a vulnerability to gain unauthorized access.
M·04exploitcvepayload
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"All the prep before this moment exists to make this moment work."
Exploitation uses a known or discovered vulnerability to gain initial code execution, data access or authentication bypass. The first time you cross the boundary into where you weren't supposed to be.
② Explanation

Categories: public exploits (ExploitDB, GitHub PoCs, Metasploit modules), web exploits (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, RCE in apps), credential-based (valid creds from spray / phish), 0-day (rare, expensive).

Quality bar: an exploit that works once and crashes the box is useless on real engagements. Prefer reliable, repeatable techniques — and always have a rollback plan if you crash a prod service.

MITRE tactic: TA0001 Initial Access + TA0002 Execution. Techniques: T1190 Exploit Public-Facing App, T1133 External Remote Services, T1078 Valid Accounts.

③ Keywords
ExploitDBCVEPoCMetasploitinitial accessRCEreverse shellreliability
④ Where It Appears
After recon + scan + enum
CVE-matching phase
Phishing payload delivery
Web app pwn
⑤ How It Works
1Match service version to CVE
2Find / write working PoC
3Test in lab if possible
4Launch against target
5Confirm access + persist immediately
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Public PoC weaponization
  • Phishing payload to user
  • Web app SQLi → RCE chain
  • Credential reuse from leaks
Defender side
  • Patch CVEs within SLA
  • EDR + AV signatures
  • Network egress filtering
  • User awareness against phishing
⑦ Remember This
The vuln is the lock. The exploit is the key. The patch is the deadbolt.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·05
Privilege Escalation
Moving from low-privilege user to root or SYSTEM.
M·05privescrootSYSTEM
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Initial access usually gets you 'nobody'. Privesc gets you 'admin'."
Privilege Escalation elevates current access — from www-data to root on Linux, from user to SYSTEM on Windows. Without this step, most foothold shells are nearly useless.
② Explanation

Linux paths: SUID/SGID binaries with shell escapes (GTFOBins), sudo misconfigs (sudo -l), kernel exploits, writable cron jobs, exposed credentials in files, capabilities on binaries.

Windows paths: unquoted service paths, weak service permissions, AlwaysInstallElevated, token impersonation (SeImpersonatePrivilege → JuicyPotato / PrintSpoofer), UAC bypass, kernel exploits.

Helpers: linpeas.sh, linenum.sh, winPEAS, PowerUp.ps1, seatbelt. They automate the 100+ enumeration checks. MITRE: TA0004 Privilege Escalation.

③ Keywords
SUIDsudolinpeaswinPEASJuicyPotatokernel exploitcroncapabilities
④ Where It Appears
After every foothold
Cloud IAM (assume-role chains)
Container escapes
AD path-to-DA
⑤ How It Works
1Enumerate as current user
2Run automated checkers
3Inspect interesting findings manually
4Pick reliable path
5Escalate + verify
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Abuse SUID + GTFOBins shell
  • Hijack writable cron job
  • Token impersonation on Windows
  • Cloud role-chain to admin
Defender side
  • Least privilege rigorously
  • Patch kernel + service binaries
  • Audit sudoers, SUID, services
  • Monitor for unusual privilege use
⑦ Remember This
A foothold is a chair. Privilege escalation is the chair becoming a throne.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·06
Persistence
Maintaining access through reboots, patches and detection sweeps.
M·06persistencebackdoorcron
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Get back in tomorrow without re-exploiting today."
Persistence is the set of techniques that ensure continued access to a compromised system. The exploit may be patched, the password rotated — but the backdoor remains.
② Explanation

Linux: cron jobs, systemd services, SSH authorized_keys, bashrc hooks, web shells in document root, rootkits (LKM, eBPF, LD_PRELOAD).

Windows: scheduled tasks, services, registry Run keys (HKCU/HKLM \Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run), WMI subscriptions, DLL hijacking, Sticky Keys backdoor.

AD: create new accounts, modify AdminSDHolder, plant Golden/Silver Tickets, DSRM password. MITRE: TA0003 Persistence — over 70 techniques.

③ Keywords
cronsystemdRun keysscheduled taskauthorized_keysGolden TicketDLL hijackWMI
④ Where It Appears
Right after privesc
Long-haul red team ops
APT campaigns
Insider threat scenarios
⑤ How It Works
1Pick blend-in mechanism
2Plant backdoor in unused path
3Test access after reboot
4Layer 2-3 different mechanisms
5Document for cleanup later
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Add SSH key to authorized_keys
  • Cron job pulling C2 every hour
  • Service that auto-restarts on boot
  • Golden Ticket forging
Defender side
  • Baseline + alert new persistence locations
  • File integrity monitoring (Wazuh, OSSEC)
  • Disable unused services
  • Rotate Kerberos krbtgt twice
⑦ Remember This
Exploitation gets you in once. Persistence is how you stay.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·07
Lateral Movement
Pivoting from one compromised host to others.
M·07lateralpivotnetwork
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"One box is a foothold. Many boxes is a campaign."
Lateral Movement spreads access across the internal network — from the initial host to file servers, domain controllers, databases, jump boxes. Where the real impact is built.
② Explanation

Windows/AD techniques: Pass-the-Hash (PtH), Pass-the-Ticket, Kerberoasting, WMI remote exec, PsExec, RDP, WinRM (evil-winrm).

Network pivoting: SSH tunnels (-L, -D), chisel, ligolo-ng, proxychains over a SOCKS proxy. Turns a single foothold into a launchpad for the rest of the LAN.

MITRE: TA0008 Lateral Movement. Techniques include T1021 Remote Services, T1550 Use Alternate Authentication Material (PtH/PtT), T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer.

③ Keywords
PtHKerberoastPsExecWinRMSSH tunnelproxychainschiselSOCKS
④ Where It Appears
Internal pentests after initial foothold
Red team campaigns
Ransomware spread phase
AD compromise chains
⑤ How It Works
1Inventory reachable hosts
2Reuse credentials / hashes
3Choose silent protocol (WMI > PsExec)
4Tunnel attacker tools through pivot
5Repeat until DA / objective
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • evil-winrm -i host -u user -H hash
  • impacket-psexec
  • SSH dynamic forward + proxychains
  • Kerberoast service accounts
Defender side
  • Network segmentation (no flat AD)
  • LAPS for local admin passwords
  • Disable NTLM where possible
  • Detect anomalous PtH / WMI
⑦ Remember This
Defense in depth is meant to make lateral movement loud and slow.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·08
Exfiltration
Stealing data from the target via covert channels.
M·08exfildatastealth
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"You got in. You looted. Now get the loot out without tripping the alarm."
Exfiltration moves sensitive data from inside the target network to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Often the actual objective — credentials, IP, PII, source code, financial data.
② Explanation

Channels: HTTPS to cloud storage (looks like normal browsing), DNS tunneling (encode data in subdomain queries — slow but very evasive), cloud sync abuse (Dropbox, OneDrive), email, encrypted ZIPs to webmail.

Encode + compress + encrypt before sending. Throttle to blend with normal traffic. Split into chunks to dodge DLP thresholds. Use legitimate domains and signed binaries to bypass URL filters.

MITRE: TA0010 Exfiltration. Techniques: T1041 Exfil Over C2, T1048 Alternative Protocol, T1567 Exfil Over Web Service, T1011 Exfil Over Physical Medium.

③ Keywords
DNS tunnelHTTPSDLPcloud abusestagingchunkingsteganographyC2
④ Where It Appears
Final phase of objective
Ransomware double-extortion
Espionage / APT campaigns
Insider threat scenarios
⑤ How It Works
1Stage data on compromised host
2Compress + encrypt
3Choose covert channel
4Throttle + chunk
5Verify off-site, wipe staging
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • DNS tunneling via dnscat2 / iodine
  • Upload to attacker S3 / Dropbox
  • Exfil over HTTPS POST
  • Steganography in images
Defender side
  • DLP on outbound traffic
  • Block unsanctioned cloud services
  • DNS query anomaly detection
  • Egress filtering + proxy inspection
⑦ Remember This
An attacker who can't take anything out has done less damage than they think.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·09
Covering Tracks
Clearing logs and removing artifacts to delay detection.
M·09antiforensicslogscleanup
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"No log, no investigation. (Until the SIEM ships logs off the host.)"
Covering Tracks erases or modifies forensic evidence of the intrusion — log entries, command history, dropped files, timestamps. The goal is to delay detection long enough to extend access or evade attribution.
② Explanation

Linux: clear ~/.bash_history (or set HISTFILE=/dev/null), edit /var/log/auth.log, wtmp, btmp. Touch files to backdate. Unset LD_PRELOAD traces. Modern systems ship logs off-host within seconds — local edits don't save you.

Windows: clear event logs (wevtutil cl Security), but this itself logs event 1102. Sophisticated: selective tampering via EventCleaner, time-stomping via SetMACE, kernel-level rootkits.

MITRE: TA0005 Defense Evasion. Techniques: T1070 Indicator Removal on Host, T1562 Impair Defenses. Pro tip: don't rely on this. Modern blue teams have remote logging.

③ Keywords
bash_historywevtutiltimestompwtmpSIEMauditdartifactT1070
④ Where It Appears
Late stage of attack
Insider trying to hide
APT long-haul ops
Malware self-cleanup
⑤ How It Works
1Identify what logs your actions
2Tamper / clear those logs
3Remove dropped tools
4Time-stomp artifacts
5Test detection still works against you
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Clear bash_history + wtmp
  • wevtutil cl Security
  • Time-stomp uploaded binaries
  • Stop + tamper auditd
Defender side
  • Remote log shipping (immutable)
  • SIEM alerts on log clear
  • File integrity monitoring
  • Tamper-evident audit logs
⑦ Remember This
If logs only live on the host, they belong to whoever owns the host.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 8 › M·10
Reporting
Documenting findings for the client — the actual product.
M·10reportdeliverableclient
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The shell wasn't the deliverable. The report is."
Reporting is the final phase — translating attack steps, evidence and impact into a document the client can act on. A great pentest with a bad report is a failure.
② Explanation

Sections: Executive Summary (1 page, business-language risk + impact, no jargon), Scope + Methodology, Findings (one per vuln: description, evidence, severity (CVSS), affected assets, reproduction steps, remediation), Appendices.

Each finding needs: clear title, CVSS v3.1 score, screenshots/PoC, exact steps to reproduce, business impact in plain English, prioritized fix recommendation. Severity should align with real exploitability, not just CVSS base.

Tooling: Dradis, Faraday, Serpico, SysReptor. Templates save 50% of the time. Keep evidence organized from day 1 — don't scramble at the end.

③ Keywords
CVSSexecutive summaryPoCremediationDradisSysReptorscoperetest
④ Where It Appears
Last phase of every engagement
Client deliverable
Compliance audits
Bug bounty submissions
⑤ How It Works
1Collect evidence as you go
2Draft executive summary
3Detail each finding with PoC
4Map severity + business impact
5Deliver + walk client through report
⑥ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Reproduce every finding cleanly
  • Provide working PoC
  • Map to MITRE ATT&CK for context
  • Suggest concrete remediation
Defender side
  • Receive report, prioritize fixes
  • Patch + verify with retest
  • Update controls based on root cause
  • Run regression scans
⑦ Remember This
The report is what the client buys. The shells are just receipts.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
9
Block 9 · Social Engineering
Hacking the Human
The cheapest, oldest and most effective attack vector — people. No CVE, no patch, no antivirus signature. Just trust, urgency, authority and the right pretext.
phishingspearpretextingbaitingvishingsmishingtailgateimpersonation
10 terms · E01 → E10 the wetware is always vulnerable
cherry root › block 9 › E·01
Phishing
Mass email to trick clicks, credentials or attachment execution.
E·01socialengemailmass
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Cast a wide net. Catch whoever bites."
Phishing sends fraudulent emails — typically impersonating trusted brands (banks, Microsoft, DocuSign, IT) — to large numbers of recipients hoping a small percentage click, log in or run an attachment.
② Explanation

Common payloads: credential harvesting (fake login page that captures username/password), malware delivery (macro-enabled docs, ISO/IMG bypassing MOTW, HTML smuggling), BEC (Business Email Compromise — invoice fraud).

Indicators: sender domain that looks off (microsft.com), generic greeting, urgent tone ("account closing in 24h"), mismatched link preview, unexpected attachment, grammar that's too clean or too broken.

Tooling: Gophish, King Phisher, EvilGoPhish, Evilginx2 (real-time MitM phishing — captures session cookies, bypasses MFA). MITRE: T1566 Phishing.

③ Keywords
BECGophishEvilginxcredential harvestMOTWDMARCSPFDKIM
④ Where It Appears
External red team start
Initial access in real breaches
Tax-season / holiday spikes
Brand impersonation campaigns
⑤ How It Works
1Pick pretext + brand to impersonate
2Register lookalike domain
3Build landing page + email
4Send to bulk list
5Capture creds / detonate payload
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Fake O365 login via Evilginx (MFA bypass)
  • Invoice PDF dropper
  • "Password reset" email to staff
  • Brand-impersonation domain
Defense awareness
  • SPF + DKIM + DMARC enforced
  • Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2)
  • User reporting button + reward
  • Email gateway URL rewriting
⑦ Remember This
Phishing isn't an email problem. It's a trust problem with email as the vector.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·02
Spear Phishing
Targeted phishing with personalized lure for one person or small group.
E·02socialengtargetedpersonalized
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Mass phishing is dynamite. Spear phishing is a sniper rifle."
Spear Phishing targets a specific individual (often C-level, finance, IT admin) with a highly personalized message based on OSINT — their boss's name, recent project, vendor relationships.
② Explanation

Recon-heavy: attacker spends hours/days reading LinkedIn, press releases, conference talks, GitHub, leaked databases. Then crafts a single email that fits the victim's context perfectly — sometimes referencing real ongoing work.

Sub-variant: whaling targets executives specifically. CEO fraud impersonates the CEO asking finance to wire money urgently. Often combined with timing (CEO traveling, can't be verified).

Click rates: generic phishing ~3%, spear phishing 30%+. The personalization defeats most user training. MITRE: T1566.001/002 Spearphishing Attachment / Link.

③ Keywords
OSINTwhalingCEO fraudBECpersonalizationpretextLinkedInDocuSign
④ Where It Appears
High-value target compromise
Nation-state campaigns
Wire-fraud BEC
Privileged-account takeover
⑤ How It Works
1Pick high-value target
2Build deep OSINT profile
3Craft tailored pretext
4Send single email at right time
5Convert click into access
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • CFO gets fake invoice from real vendor
  • CEO impersonation to wire transfer
  • Targeted MFA-bypass via Evilginx
  • Conference-follow-up lure
Defense awareness
  • Out-of-band verification for wires
  • Phishing-resistant MFA
  • Executive-protection awareness
  • Restrict who can approve transfers
⑦ Remember This
The personal touch is the payload. Verify with a phone call before you act on an email.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·03
Pretexting
Fabricated scenario to extract information or trigger action.
E·03socialengscenariotrust
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Build a story believable enough that the right answer feels like helping."
Pretexting constructs a believable scenario (the pretext) that justifies the attacker's request. Often the foundation of vishing, in-person social engineering and BEC.
② Explanation

Strong pretexts borrow real authority: "Hi, this is Mike from IT — we're rolling out the security update we sent yesterday, can you confirm your username?". Reference real internal terms, real names, real schedules to feel legitimate.

Persuasion levers: authority (boss, IT, regulator), urgency ("before the audit at 3"), scarcity, liking (chit-chat first), reciprocity, social proof ("everyone else already did it").

Famous case: Kevin Mitnick built half his career on pretexting. Modern descendant: vendor impersonation calls to helpdesks — "reset MFA for John Doe, he's locked out before a board meeting."

③ Keywords
Cialdiniauthorityurgencyhelpdeskvendorscenarioscriptrapport
④ Where It Appears
Vishing campaigns
Physical access pretexts
Helpdesk MFA-reset attacks
Vendor-impersonation fraud
⑤ How It Works
1Pick target action (info, access, money)
2Research org structure + processes
3Craft believable scenario
4Practice delivery + objection handling
5Execute on right victim at right time
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • "IT here" calling about Wi-Fi
  • Helpdesk MFA reset request
  • Vendor impersonation for invoice
  • "Auditor" requesting access list
Defense awareness
  • Callback verification on sensitive requests
  • Helpdesk procedures requiring out-of-band
  • Train staff: it's OK to verify
  • Identity-proofing for resets
⑦ Remember This
Pretexting works because helping is the default. Make verification the default instead.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·04
Baiting
Physical or digital lure — USB drops, fake downloads, free stuff.
E·04socialengphysicalusb
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Curiosity didn't kill the cat. The USB stick did."
Baiting leaves attractive bait — labeled USB drives, fake software downloads, free media — hoping a target will pick it up and run it.
② Explanation

Classic: drop USB sticks labeled "Salaries 2026" in the company parking lot. Studies show 30-60% pickup + plug-in rates. Modern variant: USB Rubber Ducky / BadUSB — looks like a stick, types pre-programmed keystrokes when plugged in.

Digital baiting: cracked software with malware, fake browser extensions, malicious npm packages with names like real ones (typosquatting), free "premium" downloads on torrents.

MITRE: T1091 Replication Through Removable Media, T1195 Supply Chain Compromise for software baiting.

③ Keywords
USB dropRubber DuckyBadUSBtyposquatcracked softwarefake extensionHIDautorun
④ Where It Appears
Red team physical assessments
Mass attacks via torrents
npm/PyPI supply chain
Conference badge giveaways
⑤ How It Works
1Pick bait that fits target context
2Embed payload (HID, executable)
3Place where target will find
4Wait for plug-in / execution
5Get callback in C2
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Rubber Ducky in branded stick
  • "Free movie" with stealer
  • Typosquat npm package
  • Conference giveaway with payload
Defense awareness
  • Disable USB autorun + HID on policy
  • Endpoint controls on removable media
  • Code-signing requirements
  • User training + report channels
⑦ Remember This
If you didn't request it and it's free, the cost is your machine.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·05
Tailgating
Physical entry by following an authorized person through a door.
E·05socialengphysicalaccess
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Hold the door for the nice person with the coffee. They were never on the badge list."
Tailgating (also piggybacking) is physically following an authorized employee through a controlled entry (badge door, mantrap, gate) without using your own credential.
② Explanation

Tricks that work: hands full (coffee + laptop), pretending to fumble for a badge, dressed as delivery / vendor / cleaning, friendly chat in a smoking area before the entry, timing for shift changes.

Variant: shoulder surfing (watching badge or PIN entry), elevator hijacking (waiting in elevator for floor swipe), vendor impersonation with fake work order.

Combats: mantraps (only one person at a time), turnstiles, badge-in/badge-out logging, security awareness — "if you don't recognize them, don't hold the door".

③ Keywords
piggybackmantrapbadgeshoulder surfvendorphysical pentestawarenessturnstile
④ Where It Appears
Physical red team
Office buildings
Data center perimeters
Healthcare / government facilities
⑤ How It Works
1Recon entry points + shift times
2Pick blend-in look
3Approach with hands full
4Follow employee through
5Walk in like you belong
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Hands-full coffee tactic
  • Delivery uniform + fake package
  • Smoke-break re-entry
  • Vendor with fake work order
Defense awareness
  • Mantraps + turnstiles
  • Visible badging policy
  • "Challenge unknowns" culture
  • Visitor escort requirements
⑦ Remember This
Physical access often beats years of digital defense. The door is part of the perimeter.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·06
Vishing
Voice phishing — social engineering over phone calls.
E·06socialengvoicephone
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Email gives you time to think. A phone call doesn't."
Vishing (voice phishing) uses phone calls to extract information, credentials or actions. Common pretexts: bank fraud team, IRS/tax authority, IT helpdesk, Microsoft support, courier delivery.
② Explanation

Why it works: phone calls feel personal + urgent, harder to think calmly, no link to inspect, caller-ID is trivially spoofed (VoIP spoofing). The attacker controls pace; victim doesn't have time to verify.

Modern twist: AI voice cloning — a 3-second sample is enough to clone someone's voice convincingly. Already used in CEO-fraud schemes for fake CFO calls authorizing transfers.

MITRE: T1566.004 Spearphishing Voice. Defense: callback policy on numbers from a verified directory, MFA codes never spoken aloud, recordings + review of suspicious calls.

③ Keywords
caller ID spoofVoIPhelpdeskIRS scamvoice cloneBECcallbackverification
④ Where It Appears
IT helpdesk attacks
Bank fraud scams
Tax-season targeted vishing
Executive impersonation
⑤ How It Works
1Pick target + pretext
2Spoof caller ID
3Call at busy / distracted moment
4Apply urgency + authority
5Extract info or trigger action
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • "Microsoft support" remote access
  • Spoofed bank fraud alert
  • Helpdesk MFA-reset trick
  • Voice-cloned CFO wire authorization
Defense awareness
  • Never share OTP / MFA over phone
  • Verify via directory callback
  • Help-desk identity-proofing
  • Train staff to hang up + call back
⑦ Remember This
Urgency on a phone call is the attacker's friend. Hang up and call back on a known number.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·07
Smishing
SMS phishing — phishing via text messages.
E·07socialengsmsmobile
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Your package couldn't be delivered. Click here. (No, it can't, because there is no package.)"
Smishing (SMS + phishing) sends fraudulent text messages with malicious links or callback numbers. Short messages, no preview, easy to spoof sender — high success rate.
② Explanation

Typical lures: parcel delivery ("USPS: address incomplete, confirm here"), bank fraud alert, MFA prompts piggybacking on real ones, tax refund, HR / payroll messages.

Mobile context is favorable to attackers: small screen hides full URL, users tap fast, mobile browsers handle URL obfuscation poorly, mobile devices often lack the EDR endpoint protection desktops have.

MITRE: T1660 Phishing (mobile-specific subtechnique). Defense: never log into your bank from an SMS link — always type the URL or use the bank app.

③ Keywords
parcelMFAshort URLmobilebrand spooftax refundHRSMS gateway
④ Where It Appears
Mass scams
Holiday-season parcel waves
Bank credential harvest
MFA-bombing combos
⑤ How It Works
1Buy SMS gateway / spoofed sender
2Build mobile-friendly phish page
3Blast to leaked phone lists
4Capture creds on tap
5Optional: combine with vishing
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • "Your package needs a fee" SMS
  • Bank fraud alert with link
  • Tax refund link
  • MFA fatigue + smishing pretext
Defense awareness
  • Don't tap links in unexpected SMS
  • Use bank apps, not SMS links
  • Report smishing to carrier (7726)
  • Phishing-resistant MFA
⑦ Remember This
Real banks don't text you links to log in. Real couriers don't ask for $2.99 redelivery fees.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·08
Watering Hole
Compromising websites the target population visits.
E·08socialengwebtargeted
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Can't get to the target? Compromise where the target drinks."
Watering Hole attacks compromise a legitimate website that the intended victims frequently visit, planting malware that triggers on visit (drive-by) or via fake updates. The target comes to you.
② Explanation

Classic targets: industry forums, niche software-download sites, vendor portals, religious or political community sites. Attacker compromises the site itself (vuln in CMS, plugin, ad-network supply chain), then plants conditional payload (only fires for specific IP/UA/geo).

High-profile examples: 2012 Council on Foreign Relations watering hole (IE 0-day), 2019 ASUS Live Update supply-chain (signed updates pushed malware), 2021 SolarWinds-adjacent campaigns.

MITRE: T1189 Drive-by Compromise. Highly favored by APT groups for targeting industries where direct phishing is risky (lots of security-aware users).

③ Keywords
drive-bysupply chainCMSpluginconditional payloadAPTad-network0-day
④ Where It Appears
APT targeting of industries
Vendor-portal compromises
Software update channels
Niche community sites
⑤ How It Works
1Profile target's browsing habits
2Find vuln site in their orbit
3Plant conditional payload
4Wait for visit
5Deliver exploit / staged loader
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Compromise industry forum CMS
  • Inject malicious JS via ad network
  • Plant fake update binary
  • Geo/IP/UA-filtered payload
Defense awareness
  • Web isolation / browser sandbox
  • Block third-party ads + script CDNs
  • Patch browsers + plugins aggressively
  • Inspect TLS at proxy for known-bad
⑦ Remember This
Sometimes the target's habits are the breach. The attacker waits where the herd drinks.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·09
Quid Pro Quo
Offering something in exchange for information or access.
E·09socialengexchangevalue
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"You give me your password. I give you tech support. Even trade, right?"
Quid Pro Quo attacks offer a service, gift or benefit in exchange for information or access. The reciprocity instinct does the work — humans feel obligated to return favors.
② Explanation

Classic example: cold-call employees claiming to be IT, offer to "fix" a slow PC if they share their password. Or offer a small gift (coffee voucher, T-shirt) in exchange for filling out a "security survey" that asks for credentials.

Modern variant: fake job interviews from real-sounding recruiters that ask the candidate to run "skill-test" code on their work laptop — often the malicious payload. Massive uptick in 2023-2024 with state-sponsored recruiting fraud.

Cialdini's reciprocity principle is the engine. Even tiny unsolicited favors create disproportionate compliance. Defense is training people to recognize the pattern.

③ Keywords
reciprocityfake interviewcold callgiftsurveyCialdinicompliancetech support
④ Where It Appears
Cold-call helpdesk scams
Fake-recruiter / interview lures
Survey-fraud
Trade-show booth giveaways
⑤ How It Works
1Pick service / gift to offer
2Identify target with relevant pain
3Make unsolicited offer
4Build rapport via small exchange
5Cash in the favor
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • "Free tech help" call asking for password
  • Fake recruiter with malicious code test
  • Survey for gift card asking creds
  • Conference giveaway phishing
Defense awareness
  • Train staff to refuse unsolicited offers
  • Never run code from interviewers
  • Verify recruiters via known channels
  • Strict cred-sharing policies
⑦ Remember This
If a stranger is being unusually helpful, ask what they're really after.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 9 › E·10
Impersonation
Pretending to be someone of authority — IT, executive, vendor, regulator.
E·10socialengidentityauthority
tags: Estudado Revisar DifÃƒÂ­cil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Don't ask. Tell. Act like you belong. People will help you do your job."
Impersonation is the broad social-engineering technique of assuming another identity — physical, voice, email or full digital — to leverage that identity's trust and access.
② Explanation

Common roles impersonated: IT helpdesk, executive (CEO/CFO BEC), vendor/contractor, regulator/auditor, law enforcement, delivery/maintenance staff. Combines easily with pretexting + vishing.

Digital impersonation includes email spoofing, lookalike domains (homograph attacks using Unicode), compromised executive accounts (real account, fake intent), AI-generated voice and video (deepfakes).

MITRE: T1656 Impersonation. Often the linchpin technique that ties together phishing, pretexting, vishing, watering-hole and physical tailgating into one campaign.

③ Keywords
BECCEO fraudhelpdeskhomographdeepfakevendorregulatorbadge clone
④ Where It Appears
BEC wire fraud
Physical pentest entries
Helpdesk MFA-resets
Vendor-portal access
⑤ How It Works
1Pick role with right authority
2Build supporting artifacts (badge, email, story)
3Approach target through right channel
4Apply authority + urgency
5Extract action / access
⑥ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • CEO email demanding urgent wire
  • Fake auditor demanding access list
  • Cloned badge + uniform
  • Deepfake video call
Defense awareness
  • Out-of-band verification policy
  • Strong identity proofing
  • Awareness of deepfake risk
  • Code words for high-value asks
⑦ Remember This
Authority is the universal lockpick. Always verify the source before you trust the role.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
10
Block 10 · Malware
The Zoo
Viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, rootkits, keyloggers, backdoors, botnets, logic bombs. Each behaves differently — different spread, different goals, different detection. Knowing the taxonomy turns "malware" from a scary word into a chess piece on the board.
viruswormtrojanransomwarespywarerootkitbotnetRAT
10 terms · X01 → X10 know the species
cherry root › block 10 › X·01
Virus
Self-replicating code that infects host files.
X·01file infectorpolymorphicmacro virus
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A virus needs a host. A worm walks alone."
A virus is malicious code that attaches itself to a legitimate host file or program and replicates when that host executes. Unlike worms, viruses cannot spread on their own — they require user action (running the file, opening a document with macros, plugging in an infected USB).
② Explanation

Viruses date back to the 1980s — Brain (1986), Michelangelo (1991), ILOVEYOU (2000). Classic types: file infectors (append code to .exe/.com), macro viruses (Office docs with VBA), boot sector viruses (infect MBR before OS loads), and polymorphic viruses that mutate their own code to evade signature detection.

Modern antivirus catches simple file infectors trivially via signatures, so attackers moved to fileless and living-off-the-land techniques. Pure viruses are now relatively rare — most modern malware blends virus, worm, and trojan traits.

MITRE ATT&CK maps execution to T1204 User Execution. Defense relies on EDR behavior analysis, application allow-listing, and macro-disable policies (Microsoft has blocked internet macros by default since 2022).

③ Keywords
file infectorpolymorphicmacro virusVBAsignatureEICART1204
④ Where It Appears
Office document macros (still common in 2024)
USB autorun in air-gapped environments
Old shareware repos
Cracked-software bundles
⑤ How It Works
1User executes infected host file
2Virus code runs alongside legit program
3Replication: virus injects itself into other files
4Payload triggers (data destruction, backdoor, etc.)
5Spreads when infected files are shared
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Macro in phishing attachment
  • Trojanized installers on warez sites
  • Infected USBs (still works on Windows w/ shortcuts)
  • Polymorphism to defeat AV signatures
Detection/mitigation
  • Signature + heuristic AV (Defender, ESET)
  • Block macros from internet by default
  • Application allow-listing (AppLocker, WDAC)
  • User education + email sandboxing
⑦ Remember This
A virus is a parasite — it cannot live without a host file.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·02
Worm
Self-propagating malware — no host needed.
X·02self-propagatingEternalBlueWannaCry
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Plug in. Get owned. No clicks required."
A worm is malware that spreads autonomously across networks by exploiting vulnerabilities or weak credentials, requiring no user interaction. The hallmark is automated propagation: one infection seeds dozens, then thousands.
② Explanation

Famous worms: Morris (1988) — the first internet worm, took down ~10% of the internet. Code Red (2001) — IIS bug. SQL Slammer (2003) — 376-byte UDP packet that doubled infections every 8.5 seconds. Conficker (2008) — exploited MS08-067, still alive in some embedded systems.

WannaCry (May 2017) and NotPetya (June 2017) are the textbook modern worms: both used the EternalBlue SMB exploit. WannaCry was ransomware-with-worm, NotPetya was wiper-as-ransomware that caused ~$10 billion in damage (Maersk, Merck, FedEx).

Worms exploit network services directly — SMB, RDP, RPC, SSH with weak creds. Patching, network segmentation, and disabling legacy protocols (SMBv1!) are the defense pillars.

③ Keywords
self-propagatingEternalBlueWannaCryNotPetyaConfickerSMBnetworkno-click
④ Where It Appears
Flat networks with unpatched Windows
IoT botnets (Mirai on Telnet/SSH)
Industrial control systems
Hospitals/critical infrastructure breaches
⑤ How It Works
1Initial infection (phish, exploit, USB)
2Scan network for vulnerable hosts
3Exploit vulnerability / weak credentials
4Drop self on new host and execute
5Loop: each new host scans further
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • SMB/EternalBlue (WannaCry, NotPetya)
  • RDP brute-force / BlueKeep
  • SSH password spraying (IoT)
  • Removable media autorun
Detection/mitigation
  • Aggressive patch management (MS17-010!)
  • Network segmentation / micro-segmentation
  • Disable SMBv1, restrict RDP
  • IDS/IPS for known worm signatures
⑦ Remember This
A worm doesn't need you to click — patch first or pay later.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·03
Trojan
Disguised as legitimate software; payload activates on run.
X·03RATbanking trojanEmotet
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"It looks like a free game. It is not a free game."
A trojan (Trojan horse) is malware that masquerades as something useful or wanted — a game, utility, document, cracked app, or system update. Unlike viruses or worms, it does not self-replicate; it relies entirely on the user installing it voluntarily.
② Explanation

Banking trojans dominate the category: Zeus, SpyEye, Emotet, TrickBot, QakBot, IcedID. Once installed, they hook the browser to steal credentials and inject fake forms during online-banking sessions ("man-in-the-browser").

RATs (Remote Access Trojans) like njRAT, DarkComet, and Quasar give the attacker full control — webcam, keystrokes, file transfer, shell. Mobile trojans (Anubis, FluBot, SharkBot) overlay banking-app screens to harvest credentials.

The distribution channels are predictable: cracked software, fake installers (notepad++ typo-squat sites), malvertising, "free" antivirus, pirated movies, and SEO poisoning of search results for popular tools.

③ Keywords
RATbanking trojanEmotetTrickBotZeusdroppermalvertisingSEO poisoning
④ Where It Appears
Pirated software on torrent sites
Fake installers via Google ads
Cracked games with bundled miners
Mobile app stores (sideloaded APKs)
⑤ How It Works
1Attacker packages malware inside legit-looking app
2Victim downloads and willingly installs
3Trojan executes payload silently
4Establishes C2 / installs persistence
5Performs theft, espionage, or further payload drop
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Cracked software bundles
  • Malvertising on streaming/warez sites
  • Fake "Adobe Flash" / "Chrome update"
  • SEO-poisoned installers (e.g. fake Notepad++)
Detection/mitigation
  • Download only from vendor sites
  • Code-signing verification
  • EDR behavioral detection
  • SmartScreen / Gatekeeper enabled
⑦ Remember This
Free software you didn't expect to be free is rarely free.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·04
Ransomware
Encrypts data, demands ransom for the decryption key.
X·04LockBitContiREvil
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Your files are encrypted. Send Bitcoin. Or don't — we sell them anyway."
Ransomware is malware that encrypts the victim's files (and often whole disks/servers) and demands payment — usually in cryptocurrency — for the decryption key. Modern variants also exfiltrate data first and threaten to publish it ("double extortion").
② Explanation

Timeline highlights: CryptoLocker (2013) popularized the model. WannaCry (2017) went global. Ryuk, Conti, REvil/Sodinokibi, LockBit, BlackCat/ALPHV, and Cl0p dominated 2019-2024 with the RaaS (Ransomware-as-a-Service) affiliate model — operators provide the malware and infrastructure, affiliates do the intrusion, profits split.

Modern intrusions follow a script: initial access (phish, RDP brute, VPN CVE) → Cobalt Strike / Mimikatz → AD domain admin → Veeam/backup destruction → mass deployment via GPO or PsExec → encryption + leak-site listing. The 2021 Colonial Pipeline (DarkSide) and 2023 MOVEit (Cl0p) campaigns are case studies.

Paying does not guarantee recovery and may be illegal (OFAC sanctioned groups). The defense priority is immutable, offline, tested backups + MFA + EDR + rapid patching.

③ Keywords
LockBitContiREvilRaaSdouble extortionCobalt StrikeBTCOFAC
④ Where It Appears
Hospital networks (CHU Brest, HSE Ireland)
Pipelines and utilities (Colonial 2021)
MSPs (Kaseya 2021)
Schools and municipalities
⑤ How It Works
1Initial access via phish, RDP, or vuln
2Reconnaissance + privilege escalation
3Backup destruction (Veeam, VSS shadows)
4Data exfiltration to leak site
5Mass encryption + ransom note
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Phishing → Emotet/QakBot → RaaS
  • Exposed RDP + weak passwords
  • VPN/edge CVEs (Ivanti, Fortinet, Citrix)
  • Supply-chain (MOVEit, Kaseya)
Detection/mitigation
  • 3-2-1 backups + immutable/offline copies
  • MFA on every external service
  • EDR with behavioral ransomware detection
  • Network segmentation + patch SLA
⑦ Remember This
Backups you have never tested to restore are not backups.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·05
Spyware
Covertly collects info — keystrokes, screen, mic, browser data.
X·05PegasusinfostealerRedLine
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"It's not paranoia if your phone really is listening."
Spyware is malware focused on covert surveillance: keylogging, screen capture, audio/video from the mic & camera, GPS, browser history, password vaults, chat content, and clipboard. Output is exfiltrated to an attacker-controlled server.
② Explanation

Commercial spyware is a multi-billion-dollar industry. NSO Group's Pegasus is the headline name — zero-click iOS/Android exploits used against journalists, dissidents, and politicians (Citizen Lab + Amnesty Tech investigations 2021-2024). FinFisher/FinSpy, Candiru, Predator (Intellexa), and Hermit occupy the same space.

Consumer-grade stalkerware (mSpy, FlexiSpy, Cocospy, KidsGuard) is marketed for "parental control" but routinely used in domestic abuse — installed by an intimate partner with brief physical access. The Coalition Against Stalkerware tracks the ecosystem.

Browser-level spyware comes as infostealers: RedLine, Vidar, Raccoon, Lumma — they harvest saved passwords, cookies, crypto wallets, and 2FA tokens in seconds, then sell the logs on dark-web markets like Russian Market.

③ Keywords
PegasusinfostealerRedLinekeyloggerstalkerwarezero-clickCitizen Labexfil
④ Where It Appears
Targeted attacks on journalists / activists
Corporate espionage
Credential theft for initial access brokers
Domestic abuse cases
⑤ How It Works
1Install via phish, exploit, or physical access
2Hook keyboard / capture screen / mic
3Stage data locally (encrypted)
4Beacon to C2 over HTTPS/Telegram/Discord
5Exfiltrate on schedule or on demand
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Zero-click iMessage / WhatsApp exploits
  • Phishing with infostealer payload
  • Cracked software (RedLine in fake installers)
  • Stalkerware via brief phone access
Detection/mitigation
  • Lockdown Mode on iOS for high-risk users
  • EDR + DNS filtering for C2
  • Full disk encryption + screen lock
  • Hardware MFA — defeats cookie theft
⑦ Remember This
If it's on, it's a microphone. If it's plugged in, it's a wiretap.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·06
Rootkit
Hides at OS/kernel level to maintain stealth access.
X·06kernelbootkitUEFI
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The best malware is the one you'll never run a tool against."
A rootkit is malware that operates at the lowest privilege levels — kernel, hypervisor, or firmware — to hide its own presence and that of other malicious components. It intercepts system calls so listings, process tables, and file enumerations lie to the user.
② Explanation

Categories by depth: user-mode (DLL injection, hooks in user APIs — easiest to detect), kernel-mode (drivers / LKMs — requires Ring 0), bootkits (infect MBR/UEFI bootloader — load before OS, e.g. LoJax, MoonBounce, BlackLotus), and firmware/SMM rootkits (survive disk wipes).

Hardware-adjacent variants: Intel ME exploitation, hard-drive firmware implants (Equation Group per Kaspersky 2015), and Thunderspy-class DMA attacks. Cloud has its own analog — hypervisor rootkits and cloud-instance metadata abuse.

Detection from a live infected system is fundamentally untrustworthy — the rootkit controls what tools see. Best practice: offline forensics, integrity monitoring (Tripwire, AIDE), Secure Boot + UEFI measured boot, and TPM-backed attestation.

③ Keywords
kernelbootkitUEFILoJaxBlackLotusSecure BootRing 0SMM
④ Where It Appears
Nation-state implants (Equation, APT41)
Persistent malware on enterprise endpoints
Compromised firmware on supply-chain devices
Hidden cryptominers on servers
⑤ How It Works
1Gain initial privileged access
2Install kernel driver / patch boot loader
3Hook system calls (file, process, network)
4Hide own files / processes / connections
5Provide backdoor channel for operator
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Signed-driver abuse (BYOVD attacks)
  • UEFI implants (LoJax, BlackLotus)
  • Hypervisor escape → host rootkit
  • Kernel CVEs + privilege escalation
Detection/mitigation
  • Secure Boot + measured boot (TPM)
  • Driver Signature Enforcement / HVCI
  • Offline scans (WinPE, Live USB)
  • Hardware root of trust (Pluton, TPM 2.0)
⑦ Remember This
A rootkit lives below your eyes — only an external view can find it.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·07
Keylogger
Records every keystroke — hardware or software.
X·07keystroke loggingSetWindowsHookExAgent Tesla
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Type your password. Now type it for the attacker too."
A keylogger captures every key pressed on a system and ships the log to an attacker. Most are software (a process or driver), but hardware variants exist — physical devices placed inline between keyboard and PC, or implanted in the keyboard.
② Explanation

Software keyloggers hook keyboard APIs (SetWindowsHookEx on Windows, /dev/input on Linux, Accessibility APIs on macOS/Android). Many infostealers (RedLine, Agent Tesla, FormBook) include a keylogging module. They typically also grab clipboard contents and screenshots.

Hardware keyloggers (KeyGrabber, KeyLlama, KeyCobra) are USB pass-through devices the size of a thumb-drive — they need brief physical access for placement and retrieval. PS/2 inline versions also exist for older systems. ATM skimmers are a related cousin.

Acoustic / electromagnetic side-channels: research shows keyboards can sometimes be reconstructed from microphone audio or EM emanations (TEMPEST). On phones, accelerometer-based keystroke inference has been demonstrated.

Defense: hardware MFA tokens (FIDO2/WebAuthn) defeat keyloggers entirely — they sign challenges, not type secrets. Password managers with autofill avoid typing the password at all.

③ Keywords
keystroke loggingSetWindowsHookExAgent TeslahardwareTEMPESTclipboard grabside-channelinfostealer
④ Where It Appears
Banking-trojan modules
Public/shared computers (cybercafés, libraries)
Corporate espionage on shared workstations
Domestic surveillance
⑤ How It Works
1Install via phish / RAT / physical access
2Hook keyboard input layer
3Log keys + window titles + timestamps
4Optionally screenshot on focus change
5Exfiltrate via SMTP / HTTPS / Telegram
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Bundled with RATs (njRAT, Quasar)
  • Standalone infostealer (Agent Tesla)
  • USB hardware loggers (insider threat)
  • Malicious browser extensions (form-grab)
Detection/mitigation
  • FIDO2 hardware keys (no typed password)
  • Password manager autofill
  • Inspect USB ports for inline devices
  • EDR detects keyboard-hook abuse
⑦ Remember This
If you can avoid typing the password, the keylogger never sees it.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·08
Backdoor
Hidden access mechanism bypassing normal authentication.
X·08web shellCobalt StrikeSliver
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Lock the front door. Then check the basement window."
A backdoor is any concealed channel that allows access while bypassing normal authentication. It can be malware-installed, intentionally placed by a developer/vendor, or the byproduct of a deliberate supply-chain compromise.
② Explanation

Threat-actor backdoors: web shells (China Chopper, c99.php, ASPXSpy) dropped onto web servers; implants like Cobalt Strike Beacon, Sliver, Brute Ratel, Empire; and persistent loaders (Sunburst in the 2020 SolarWinds attack — 18,000 victims).

Vendor / developer backdoors: hard-coded credentials in IoT devices and consumer routers, "service accounts" left enabled, and historical NSA-suspected weakening (Dual_EC_DRBG, Juniper ScreenOS 2015). The xz-utils backdoor (CVE-2024-3094, March 2024) was a years-long social-engineering supply-chain operation discovered by accident — a textbook nation-state-level effort.

Backdoors hide in scheduled tasks, services, registry Run keys, cron, systemd units, SSH authorized_keys, IIS modules, and signed-driver loaders. Hunting requires baseline + diff plus EDR telemetry, not just AV.

③ Keywords
web shellCobalt StrikeSliverSolarWindsxz-utilshard-coded credsauthorized_keysC2
④ Where It Appears
Compromised web servers (china chopper)
Supply-chain attacks (SolarWinds, 3CX, xz)
IoT devices with vendor admin accounts
Long-term APT persistence
⑤ How It Works
1Exploit / phish / supply-chain compromise
2Drop backdoor mechanism
3Hide it (legit name, signed binary, system path)
4Wait for / initiate operator connection
5Re-enter at will, even after credentials change
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Web shells on RCE-vuln web apps
  • C2 implants (Cobalt Strike, Sliver)
  • Signed supply-chain payloads
  • Hardcoded vendor creds in firmware
Detection/mitigation
  • Integrity monitoring of web roots
  • EDR + network egress filtering
  • Audit scheduled tasks / services / cron
  • SBOM + signed-update verification
⑦ Remember This
A backdoor doesn't need to break a lock — it was installed by the locksmith.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·09
Botnet
Network of compromised devices controlled remotely.
X·09MiraiEmotetC2
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"One bot is a nuisance. A million bots is a country.";
A botnet is a fleet of compromised devices (PCs, servers, IoT) that obey commands from an attacker-controlled C2 server. They are rented out for DDoS, spam, credential stuffing, click-fraud, crypto-mining, proxying, and ransomware deployment.
② Explanation

IoT botnets: Mirai (2016) infected ~600k cameras and routers via default Telnet credentials; its source code was published and forked into Mozi, Hajime, Meris, and dozens more. Mirai variants powered the 2016 attack on Dyn that took down Twitter, Reddit, Spotify, and Netflix.

PC botnets: Zeus/GameOver, Emotet (taken down by Europol in Jan 2021, rebuilt later), TrickBot, QakBot, Necurs, Conficker. Many serve as loaders — first stage that resells access to ransomware affiliates.

C2 channels evolved from IRC → HTTP/HTTPS → DNS tunneling → domain generation algorithms (DGA) → fast-flux DNS → P2P → Tor hidden services → Telegram/Discord. Sinkhole + takedown operations are the main defensive lever, alongside ISP egress filtering and IoT vendor accountability.

③ Keywords
MiraiEmotetC2DDoSDGAsinkholeIoTcredential stuffing
④ Where It Appears
DDoS-for-hire (booter / stresser) services
Email spam campaigns
Credential-stuffing attacks
Cryptojacking on hijacked servers
⑤ How It Works
1Infect device (worm, weak creds, exploit)
2Bot registers with C2
3C2 issues commands (attack, mine, proxy)
4Devices execute in parallel
5Operator monetizes (rent / use directly)
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Default Telnet/SSH creds (Mirai-class)
  • Phishing + macro → Emotet loader
  • Unpatched router CVEs
  • Worm-style SMB / RPC spreading
Detection/mitigation
  • Change default IoT credentials
  • Egress filtering at network edge
  • DNS sinkholing of known C2
  • Coordinated international takedowns
⑦ Remember This
Your fridge isn't hacking the Pentagon — but it is helping someone else try.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 10 › X·10
Logic Bomb
Triggers on a specific event, date, or condition.
X·10insider threattime bombtrigger condition
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"It sits and waits. Until the day it doesn't."
A logic bomb is malicious code that lies dormant until a specific condition is met — a date, a missing employee record, a particular file existing, a counter reaching a threshold — at which point it activates its payload.
② Explanation

Famous cases: the Roger Duronio / UBS PaineWebber (2002) insider — a sysadmin planted a logic bomb that wiped 2,000 servers when triggered; he got 8 years in federal prison. The Omega Engineering (1996) case — Tim Lloyd's logic bomb destroyed software worth $10M after he was fired. The Fannie Mae (2008) attempted bomb — Rajendrasinh Makwana scheduled a script to wipe ~4,000 servers; caught before execution.

Logic bombs are often insider attacks — disgruntled employees with legitimate access who hide trigger conditions ("if my employee ID is no longer in HR database, run wipe"). They are also a final-stage payload in some malware (e.g. NotPetya's bootloader wipe was effectively a logic bomb keyed to encryption completion).

Detection is genuinely hard — the malicious code does nothing observable until trigger. Defense is code review, four-eyes principle on production changes, version control discipline, separation of duties, and aggressive offboarding (revoke all access on day of termination, not later).

③ Keywords
insider threattime bombtrigger conditionDuronioUBScode reviewseparation of dutiesoffboarding
④ Where It Appears
Disgruntled employee scenarios
Sabotage by departing developers
Latent malware payloads
Vendor / contractor misconduct
⑤ How It Works
1Attacker writes triggered payload
2Embed in legit codebase or scheduled task
3Define trigger (date, condition, absence of file)
4Wait — code is dormant and silent
5Condition met → payload executes
⑥ Attack vs Defense
How it spreads/works
  • Insider commits hidden code
  • Trigger on user-not-found-in-HR
  • Date-based wipe scripts
  • Conditional malware payloads
Detection/mitigation
  • Mandatory code review (4-eyes)
  • Separation of duties (dev ≠ deploy)
  • Immediate offboarding access revocation
  • Audit scheduled tasks / cron / triggers
⑦ Remember This
The most dangerous malware is the one that hasn't done anything yet.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
11
Block 11 · Defense
The Stack
Defense is layered, not perimeter. Firewalls, IDS/IPS, SIEM, EDR, WAF, honeypots, patching, backups, IR plans, and trained humans — each one buys time. Together they buy resilience. No single control catches everything; defense in depth is how you survive when one fails.
firewallIDSSIEMEDRWAFhoneypotpatchbackupIRtraining
10 terms · D01 → D10 defense in depth
cherry root › block 11 › D·01
Firewall
Filters network traffic based on rules — stateful vs stateless.
D·01statefulNGFWiptables
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Default deny is not paranoia. It is hygiene."
A firewall enforces a policy on traffic between network zones — typically allow/deny based on source, destination, port, and protocol. Stateless firewalls evaluate each packet alone; stateful firewalls track connections so return traffic of an allowed session is auto-permitted.
② Explanation

Generations: packet filter (1st gen, e.g. early Cisco ACLs) → stateful (2nd gen, e.g. Check Point FW-1, iptables/nftables) → application-layer proxy (3rd gen) → NGFW / Next-Gen Firewall (Palo Alto, Fortinet FortiGate, Check Point, Cisco Firepower) which adds deep packet inspection, application identification (App-ID), user identity (User-ID), IPS, and TLS interception.

Host-based firewalls (Windows Defender Firewall, iptables/nftables, pf on BSD/macOS, ufw for ubuntu users) live on each endpoint; perimeter firewalls live at network boundaries. Cloud equivalents: AWS Security Groups (stateful, instance-level), NACLs (stateless, subnet-level), Azure NSGs, GCP VPC firewall rules.

Modern doctrine: assume the perimeter is porous and add Zero Trust — every connection re-authenticated and re-authorized, regardless of network location.

③ Keywords
statefulNGFWiptablespfPalo AltoSecurity Groupdefault-denyZero Trust
④ Where It Appears
Network perimeter (DMZ design)
Cloud VPC / security groups
Host hardening (Windows Defender FW)
Segmentation between dev/prod
⑤ How It Works
1Define security zones (trust levels)
2Write default-deny ruleset
3Allow only required services explicitly
4Log denies + monitor anomalies
5Review and prune rules regularly
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Tunneling over allowed ports (443, 53)
  • Compromise an allowed source IP
  • Lateral movement inside the trust zone
  • VPN/edge appliance CVEs
Best practices
  • Default-deny + explicit allow-list
  • Egress filtering (not just ingress)
  • Segmentation: VLANs / micro-seg
  • Log to SIEM + alert on rule violations
⑦ Remember This
Allow-list what you need. Deny everything else by default.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·02
IDS / IPS
Intrusion Detection (passive) and Prevention (active blocking) Systems.
D·02SnortSuricataZeek
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"IDS tells you. IPS does something about it."
An IDS (Intrusion Detection System) monitors traffic / host activity and alerts on suspicious patterns — it is passive. An IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) sits inline and can drop / reset / block detected attacks in real time.
② Explanation

Network-based (NIDS/NIPS) examples: Snort and Suricata (signature + protocol analysis, open source), Zeek (formerly Bro — protocol-aware traffic analysis, not signature-driven). Commercial: Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Threat Prevention, Fortinet FortiGuard IPS.

Host-based (HIDS): OSSEC/Wazuh, Tripwire, AIDE — log analysis, file integrity, rootkit checks. EDR products subsume much of this on modern endpoints.

Detection approaches: signature-based (catches known threats, misses zero-day), anomaly-based (learns baseline, alerts on deviation — high false-positive rate), behavioral (sequences of actions). Modern stacks combine all three plus threat-intel feeds (e.g. Emerging Threats, AlienVault OTX, MISP).

Trade-off: IPS in blocking mode can break legit traffic on false positives. Many shops run IPS in detect-only on critical paths.

③ Keywords
SnortSuricataZeeksignaturesanomalyWazuhSOCET Open
④ Where It Appears
Perimeter network monitoring
Detecting C2 beaconing
Web-attack detection (SQLi, XSS patterns)
Compliance requirements (PCI DSS)
⑤ How It Works
1Place sensor inline (IPS) or on tap (IDS)
2Load signatures + tune for environment
3Match traffic against rules / models
4Generate alerts to SIEM / SOC
5IPS additionally drops / resets / quarantines
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Encrypted traffic without TLS inspection
  • Slow-and-low scans (under thresholds)
  • Polymorphic payloads (defeats signatures)
  • Living-off-the-land (legit tools)
Best practices
  • Tune rules — kill the noise
  • Feed alerts into SIEM + SOAR
  • Combine signature + anomaly + behavior
  • Threat-intel updates daily (ET, vendor feeds)
⑦ Remember This
Alerts no one reads are not detection — they are decoration.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·03
SIEM
Centralized log aggregation, correlation, and alerting.
D·03SplunkSentinelELK
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"Logs everywhere or logs nowhere — anything else is theater."
A SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) ingests logs from every source (endpoints, servers, network gear, cloud, apps), normalizes them, correlates events across sources, and alerts on patterns indicative of attack.
② Explanation

Major platforms: Splunk Enterprise Security, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, Elastic Security (ELK stack), Sumo Logic, Chronicle (Google), Exabeam, Wazuh (open source). The market is consolidating with XDR convergence.

Inputs: Windows Event Logs, syslog, firewall/IDS, EDR telemetry, cloud audit logs (CloudTrail, Azure Activity, GCP Audit), DNS, proxy, AD/Entra, identity provider logs (Okta, Auth0), application logs. Frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK mapping of detections is now standard, often expressed as Sigma rules (vendor-neutral detection language).

SIEM is famously expensive — pricing on data volume (GB/day) drives architectural choices (filtering at source, tiered storage, hot/cold separation). A common pattern: cheap data lake (S3 / Snowflake) + SIEM for the hot tier. SOAR layers on top to automate response playbooks.

③ Keywords
SplunkSentinelELKcorrelationSigmaSOARMITRE ATT&CKlog pipeline
④ Where It Appears
SOC central console
Compliance reporting (PCI, SOX, HIPAA)
Threat hunting platform
Incident timeline reconstruction
⑤ How It Works
1Ship logs from all sources to SIEM
2Normalize / parse into common schema
3Apply correlation rules (Sigma, custom)
4Generate prioritized alerts
5Investigate / hunt / respond via SOAR
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Clear / disable logging on compromised host
  • Use legit admin tools (low SIEM signal)
  • Encrypted C2 hides payload from inspection
  • Volume of false positives → alert fatigue
Best practices
  • Centralize logs off-host (write-once)
  • Detection-as-code (Sigma in git, CI tests)
  • Tune alerts — track MTTD/MTTR metrics
  • SOAR for repeatable response
⑦ Remember This
If you don't centralize the log, the attacker will delete it.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·04
EDR
Endpoint Detection & Response — visibility, hunt, respond.
D·04CrowdStrikeDefenderSentinelOne
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"AV asks what. EDR asks how, when, by whom, and where it went next."
EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) is the evolution of antivirus — instead of just blocking known-bad files, it continuously records process trees, network connections, file activity, registry changes, and user actions on endpoints, then detects attack patterns and supports remote investigation and isolation.
② Explanation

Leaders (Gartner MQ): CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne Singularity, Palo Alto Cortex XDR, Sophos Intercept X, Trend Vision One, Bitdefender GravityZone. Open-ish stack: Wazuh + osquery + Sysmon.

Capabilities: real-time behavioral detection (no signature needed for unknown threats), full process-tree replay, network containment / isolation of a single host without unplugging it, remote shell for IR, deception (canary tokens / fake creds), and tight MITRE ATT&CK mapping. XDR extends EDR with email, identity, and cloud telemetry in one console.

Limitations: kernel-level EDR drivers can themselves cause outages — the July 2024 CrowdStrike Falcon update incident bricked ~8.5M Windows machines globally. Defense in depth still matters; EDR is one layer, not the whole stack.

③ Keywords
CrowdStrikeDefenderSentinelOneXDRSysmonosquerybehavioralisolate
④ Where It Appears
Modern enterprise endpoint security
Threat hunting / IR investigation
Ransomware behavioral blocking
MDR (managed detection) services
⑤ How It Works
1Deploy agent on every endpoint
2Stream telemetry to cloud / SIEM
3Detect via behavior + AI + signatures
4Alert + auto-isolate suspicious host
5Analyst hunts / investigates / responds
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • EDR kill / unhook (BYOVD: bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver)
  • Living-off-the-land (PowerShell, certutil, rundll32)
  • Process injection into trusted binaries
  • Tampering with EDR service / agent
Best practices
  • Tamper protection enabled
  • Application allow-listing complements EDR
  • Hunt regularly — don't wait for alerts
  • Cloud-managed updates with staged rollout
⑦ Remember This
EDR sees the verbs, not just the nouns — that's the whole point.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·05
WAF
Web Application Firewall — Layer 7 filtering for HTTP traffic.
D·05OWASP CRSModSecurityCloudflare
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A WAF buys you time. It does not fix your code."
A WAF (Web Application Firewall) inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic and blocks requests matching known web-attack patterns — SQLi, XSS, LFI, RCE, command injection, suspicious headers, bot signatures.
② Explanation

Players: Cloudflare WAF, AWS WAF, Akamai Kona/App & API Protector, F5 Advanced WAF / NGINX App Protect, Imperva, Fastly Next-Gen WAF (Signal Sciences), Azure Front Door WAF. Open source: ModSecurity + OWASP Core Rule Set (CRS) — the de-facto baseline ruleset.

Modes: blocking (drops malicious requests), detection-only (logs but allows — used during tuning), and challenge (CAPTCHA / JS challenge for suspicious). Modern WAFs add bot management, API schema validation, rate limiting, and ML-based anomaly detection. Cloud WAFs are usually combined with DDoS protection (Cloudflare, AWS Shield).

WAFs are routinely bypassed — encoding tricks, HTTP request smuggling, parser confusion, novel injection patterns. Treat the WAF as a safety net, not a fix: secure the code, then add the WAF for defense in depth and zero-day window mitigation.

③ Keywords
OWASP CRSModSecurityCloudflareAWS WAFbot managementrate limitAPI protectionvirtual patch
④ Where It Appears
Front of every public web app / API
PCI DSS 6.6 compliance
Virtual patching pre-deployment of fix
DDoS L7 mitigation
⑤ How It Works
1Place WAF in front of web app (reverse proxy / CDN)
2Enable baseline ruleset (OWASP CRS)
3Tune for false positives on real traffic
4Switch from detect to block
5Monitor + add custom rules per app
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Encoding tricks (Unicode, double URL-encode)
  • HTTP request smuggling
  • Origin IP discovery (direct hit bypassing WAF)
  • Novel payloads not in signatures
Best practices
  • Hide origin server (only WAF can reach it)
  • Keep OWASP CRS + custom rules in git
  • Test deploys with detect-mode first
  • Combine with rate limiting + bot mgmt
⑦ Remember This
Fix the code first. A WAF is a bandage, not surgery.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·06
Honeypot
Decoy systems to attract, observe, and study attackers.
D·06CowrieDionaeaT-Pot
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The only system you don't mind getting hacked."
A honeypot is a deliberately exposed, instrumented decoy with no legitimate purpose — any interaction with it is suspicious by definition. It exists to lure attackers, study their TTPs, and serve as an early-warning trip-wire.
② Explanation

Interaction levels: low-interaction (emulates services, easy to deploy and safe — Cowrie for SSH/Telnet, Dionaea for malware capture, Glastopf for web), medium, and high-interaction (real OSes in sandboxes — richer data, higher risk).

Specialized variants: honeytokens (decoy credentials, fake API keys, dummy database records that alert when used — Canarytokens.org, Thinkst Canary), honey users in Active Directory, honeyfiles on shares. The T-Pot all-in-one platform packages many sensors. Project Honey Pot tracks spammers and harvesters at internet scale.

Defensive ROI: honeypots produce extremely high-fidelity alerts — any login to "backup-admin" with the fake password is an attacker. They are an underused, cheap detection layer especially inside the network for catching lateral movement.

③ Keywords
CowrieDionaeaT-Potcanary tokenThinkstdeceptionhoney-userlateral movement
④ Where It Appears
Internal network deception
Threat intel collection
Detecting lateral movement
Researcher / academic studies
⑤ How It Works
1Deploy decoy service / token
2Make it discoverable (DNS, shares, etc.)
3Instrument: log every interaction
4Alert on any access — high fidelity
5Analyze captured TTPs / IOCs
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Fingerprint to detect honeypot (Cowrie patterns)
  • Avoid "too easy" targets (suspicious)
  • Sandbox-aware malware (won't detonate)
  • Time-delayed payloads
Best practices
  • Mix decoys with real assets (blend in)
  • Place honeytokens inside real DBs / shares
  • Wire alerts straight to SOC
  • Rotate decoys + token names periodically
⑦ Remember This
If anyone touches the honeypot, it is not a false positive.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·07
Patch Management
Tracking and deploying vendor fixes — the unglamorous superpower.
D·07Patch TuesdayCVEKEV
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The CVE was published. The patch was published. You did not apply it. That's on you."
Patch management is the discipline of inventorying assets, tracking vendor advisories and CVEs, testing fixes, and deploying them across the estate within a defined SLA — for OS, firmware, applications, libraries, and containers.
② Explanation

Cadences and players: Patch Tuesday (Microsoft, 2nd Tuesday monthly), Oracle quarterly CPU, Cisco semiannual + emergency, Linux distros rolling. Tooling: WSUS / Intune / SCCM for Windows, Ivanti, Tanium, ManageEngine, Automox, BigFix. Linux: package managers + Spacewalk/Satellite, Ansible. Container: image rebuilds, not in-place patching.

Real-world disasters from missed patches: Equifax (2017) — unpatched Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638, 147M records. WannaCry (2017) — MS17-010, patched 2 months prior. MOVEit (2023), Log4Shell (Dec 2021), Ivanti VPN (2024), Citrix Bleed (2023) — all weaponized within days of disclosure.

Prioritization frameworks: CVSS score, CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) catalog — patch KEV-listed CVEs within mandated timeframes, EPSS (exploit prediction). Risk-based, not bulk.

③ Keywords
Patch TuesdayCVEKEVCVSSEPSSWSUSIntuneSLA
④ Where It Appears
Enterprise endpoint compliance
PCI / HIPAA / ISO requirements
Critical infrastructure / OT updates
Cloud workload (golden image rebuilds)
⑤ How It Works
1Inventory every asset (CMDB)
2Subscribe to vendor advisories + CISA KEV
3Test patches in staging
4Deploy via rings (canary → broad)
5Verify + report compliance
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Race attackers to weaponize new CVEs (days, not months)
  • Target n-day vulns on unpatched fleets
  • Exploit edge appliances (often last patched)
  • Supply-chain CVEs (libraries, containers)
Best practices
  • Define SLA: KEV in 14 days, critical in 30
  • Automate where possible (Intune, Ansible)
  • Patch edge / VPN / hypervisor first
  • Track MTTP (mean time to patch) as KPI
⑦ Remember This
Most breaches use vulns over 1 year old. Patch faster than the headline.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·08
Backup Strategy
3-2-1 rule, offline copies, and tested restores.
D·083-2-1immutableair-gap
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"A backup you have never restored from is a hope, not a backup."
A backup strategy ensures data can be recovered after loss, corruption, or ransomware. The classic 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies of data, on 2 different media, with 1 off-site. Modern variant 3-2-1-1-0: add 1 immutable/offline copy, 0 errors on restore tests.
② Explanation

Ransomware reshaped backups. Attackers now actively destroy backups before encrypting — Veeam servers are a top target, VSS shadow copies are deleted (vssadmin delete shadows /all), cloud-sync'd backups are encrypted with the rest. The defense: immutable backups (S3 Object Lock, WORM tape, Veeam Hardened Repository, Wasabi/Backblaze immutability), air-gapped copies, and separate auth domains for backup infrastructure.

Concepts: RPO (Recovery Point Objective — how much data loss is tolerable, sets backup frequency), RTO (Recovery Time Objective — how fast you must be back). Backup types: full, incremental, differential, synthetic full, continuous (CDP).

Tools: Veeam, Commvault, Rubrik, Cohesity, Dell Avamar/Networker, Veritas NetBackup; cloud-native AWS Backup, Azure Backup, GCP Backup & DR. The single most-skipped step: quarterly full-restore tests from offline media.

③ Keywords
3-2-1immutableair-gapRPORTOVeeamObject Lockrestore drill
④ Where It Appears
Ransomware recovery
Hardware failure
Accidental deletion
Compliance retention
⑤ How It Works
1Identify critical data + RPO/RTO
2Implement 3-2-1(-1-0) topology
3Encrypt + immutable + off-site
4Schedule + monitor every job
5TEST RESTORES on a schedule
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Destroy Veeam server + repository
  • vssadmin delete shadows
  • Encrypt cloud-sync'd backup folders
  • Steal backup-admin credentials
Best practices
  • Immutable repository (S3 Object Lock, Veeam Hardened)
  • Separate auth domain for backup admin
  • Offline / air-gapped tier
  • Quarterly restore tests — measure RTO
⑦ Remember This
Backups protect against ransomware only if attackers cannot delete them.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·09
Incident Response
Prep / Identify / Contain / Eradicate / Recover / Lessons Learned.
D·09PICERLNIST 800-61DFIR
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"In a crisis, you don't rise to the occasion — you fall to your level of preparation."
Incident Response (IR) is the structured process of handling a confirmed or suspected security incident. The classic SANS / NIST 6-phase model: Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned.
② Explanation

Phase 1 Preparation: IR plan, runbooks, retainers, contact lists, jump kit, war-room setup, tabletop exercises. Phase 2 Identification: triage alerts, scope, classify severity (P1-P4), declare incident. Phase 3 Containment: short-term (isolate host) and long-term (rebuild, segment).
