Phase 4 Eradication: remove malware, close access, rotate creds. Phase 5 Recovery: restore from clean backups, monitor, validate. Phase 6 Lessons Learned: post-mortem within 2 weeks, action items tracked.

Key roles: IR lead, comms lead, technical lead, legal, executive sponsor. External help: DFIR retainers (Mandiant, CrowdStrike Services, Unit 42, Kroll, Coveware for ransomware negotiation). Frameworks: NIST SP 800-61, SANS PICERL, MITRE ATT&CK for technique mapping.

Disclosure obligations now bite: SEC Rule (Dec 2023) — 4 business days for material cyber incidents at US-listed companies; GDPR — 72 hours; NIS2 in EU; varying US state breach-notification laws.

③ Keywords
PICERLNIST 800-61DFIRplaybooktabletopretainerdisclosurepost-mortem
④ Where It Appears
Ransomware outbreak
Data breach investigation
Insider threat case
Supply-chain compromise response
⑤ How It Works
1Preparation: plan, runbooks, retainers
2Identification: triage + classify
3Containment: isolate, preserve evidence
4Eradication: remove attacker, close gaps
5Recovery: restore + monitor
6Lessons Learned: post-mortem + actions
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Wipe logs to slow forensics
  • Multiple footholds (so cleanup misses one)
  • Time attack to weekends / holidays
  • Threaten leak to pressure quick payment
Best practices
  • Tabletop exercises 2x year
  • Pre-signed DFIR retainer
  • Out-of-band comms (Signal, separate domain)
  • Practice the runbook — don't just write it
⑦ Remember This
An incident response plan you've never rehearsed will fail when you need it.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente
cherry root › block 11 › D·10
Security Awareness Training
Human-firewall: phishing tests, education, culture.
D·10KnowBe4phishing simulationBEC
tags: Estudado Revisar Difícil Favorito
① Simple Definition
"The user is not the weakest link. The untrained user is."
Security Awareness Training turns users into an active defense layer — teaching how to spot phishing, handle data, report incidents, and resist social engineering, then measuring it with simulated attacks.
② Explanation

Platforms: KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness (formerly Wombat), SANS Security Awareness, Hoxhunt, Cofense PhishMe, Mimecast, Microsoft Attack Simulation Training. The pattern: short modules + periodic phishing simulations + dashboards.

Effective programs share traits: positive reinforcement over shame, reporting (a one-click "Report Phish" Outlook button) is the headline metric — not click-rate alone, frequent short content (5 minutes monthly beats 1 hour annually), role-based modules (devs get secure-coding, finance gets BEC/wire-fraud), and just-in-time teaching when someone clicks a simulation.

Common pitfalls: punitive culture suppresses reporting, simulations that punch down (mock layoff emails, fake bonuses) destroy trust, and one-and-done annual videos that everyone clicks through. The goal is culture, not compliance check-box.

③ Keywords
KnowBe4phishing simulationBECreport-phishculturejust-in-timeBECcompliance
④ Where It Appears
Annual compliance (PCI, HIPAA, ISO 27001)
Anti-phishing programs
New-hire security onboarding
Role-based training (devs, finance, execs)
⑤ How It Works
1Baseline phishing click-rate
2Deliver short modules monthly
3Run varied phishing simulations
4Coach clickers (not punish)
5Track reporting rate as primary KPI
⑥ Attack vs Defense
Where attackers try to bypass
  • Highly targeted spear-phish / BEC
  • AI-generated voice/video (deepfake fraud)
  • Off-hours pretext (urgent CEO request)
  • QR codes / "quishing" (bypass URL filters)
Best practices
  • 1-click report button + visible thanks
  • Just-in-time teaching on click
  • Role-based + frequent + short
  • Measure REPORT-RATE, not just clicks
⑦ Remember This
Train people to report fast — beats training them to never click.
Minhas Notas
Salvo automaticamente

Manage Progress

Download all your tags, notes, goals and quiz scores as a single .cherry.json file.

cherry root › resources › CTFs
CTFs for Beginners
Where to actually practice — picked platforms ranked by friendliness.
Recommended order ✦  If you've never done a CTF: PicoCTF → OverTheWire Bandit → TryHackMe paths. Start free, build muscle memory, then move into boxes.
PicoCTFBeginnerpicoctf.org ↗
Carnegie Mellon's flagship beginner CTF. Year-round practice, US high-school-focused but open to anyone. Strong web/crypto/forensics intro.
✦ Best first stop. Free, no install, browser-based.
OverTheWire — BanditBeginneroverthewire.org/wargames/bandit ↗
Pure SSH wargame teaching Linux fundamentals one level at a time. 34 levels, terminal-only, no GUI.
✦ Do this before anything else if you're shaky on the shell.
TryHackMeBeginnertryhackme.com ↗
Gamified rooms with guided learning paths. In-browser AttackBox or your own VPN. Generous free tier, paid unlocks more rooms.
✦ Pre-Security & Complete Beginner paths are the gold standard.
HackTheBoxIntermediatehackthebox.com ↗
Real-world style boxes — less hand-holding, OSCP-flavoured. Starting Point series gives a softer onramp; retired machines come with writeups.
✦ Graduate here once you can solve THM rooms unaided.
Root-MeIntermediateroot-me.org ↗
400+ challenges across web, app, crypto, forensic, network, realist. French/English bilingual. Strong on app reversing & web specifics.
✦ Great for grinding a single category until it clicks.
CTFtimeAdvancedctftime.org ↗
Calendar & ranking of live competitive CTFs. Find weekend events, team up, read writeups of past challenges.
✦ When you're ready to compete on real timed events.
cherry root › resources › THM trail
TryHackMe Trail
An ordered path through TryHackMe — from zero to OSCP-prep.
Follow the trail top-to-bottom. Each stage assumes the previous. Estimated times are full-path completion at a comfortable pace.
1 · Pre-Security Path
Cyber 101 — no prior knowledge needed.
Network FundamentalsBeginner · 4h
Linux Fundamentals 1, 2, 3Beginner · 6h
Windows Fundamentals 1, 2, 3Beginner · 5h
Security Principles & CareersBeginner · 2h
2 · Complete Beginner Path
First taste of hacking — small CTF rooms with hand-holding.
Linux Modules & Web FundamentalsBeginner · 5h
Networking Modules (nmap, recon)Beginner · 6h
Web Hacking FundamentalsBeginner · 7h
Burp Suite — BasicsBeginner · 3h
OWASP Top 10 (2021)Beginner · 8h
3 · Jr Penetration Tester Path
Methodology, real tooling, full kill chain practice.
Pentesting Fundamentals & EthicsEasy · 4h
Reconnaissance & EnumerationEasy · 8h
Vulnerability Research & ScanningEasy · 6h
Exploitation Basics — MetasploitEasy · 7h
Privilege Escalation (Linux + Win)Medium · 10h
4 · CompTIA Security+
Exam prep — broader security knowledge, certification-aligned.
Threats, Attacks & VulnerabilitiesEasy · 8h
Architecture & DesignEasy · 6h
Implementation, Ops & GovernanceMedium · 9h
5 · Offensive Pentesting Path
OSCP-style — harder boxes, less guidance.
Active Directory BasicsMedium · 6h
Advanced Exploitation & Buffer OverflowHard · 10h
Network Pivoting & TunnelingHard · 8h
OSCP-style boxes (Steel Mountain, Kenobi, Mr Robot)Hard · 20h+
cherry root › cheatsheet › nmap
nmap Cheatsheet
Host discovery, scan types, NSE, evasion — one dense page.
Host Discovery
-snping sweep — no port scan
-sPlegacy alias for -sn
-Pnskip discovery — assume host up
-PRARP ping (LAN)
-PE / -PP / -PMICMP echo / timestamp / netmask
--top-ports Nscan most common N ports
Scan Types
-sSSYN stealth (default as root)
-sTfull TCP connect (unprivileged)
-sUUDP scan
-sAACK — map firewall rules
-sF / -sN / -sXFIN / NULL / Xmas stealth
-sVservice version detection
-OOS fingerprint
-Aaggressive: -O -sV -sC --traceroute
Port Selection
-p 80single port
-p-all 65535 ports
-p 1-1000range
-p T:80,U:53mixed TCP/UDP
--top-ports 100most common 100
Timing
-T0paranoid (IDS evasion)
-T1sneaky
-T2polite
-T3normal (default)
-T4aggressive — common in CTF
-T5insane — packet loss likely
--min-rate 1000force minimum packets/sec
--max-rate 500cap packets/sec
Output
-oN filenormal human-readable
-oX fileXML — feed to other tools
-oG filegrepable
-oA baseall three formats at once
-v / -vvverbose / very verbose
NSE Scripts
--script defaultsafe default scripts (-sC)
--script vulnvulnerability checks
--script "http-*"all HTTP scripts
--script-args k=vpass arguments to scripts
smb-os-discoveryWindows version via SMB
ssl-enum-ciphersTLS cipher suite audit
http-enumweb app fingerprint
dns-brutesubdomain brute-force
Evasion
-ffragment packets
-D RND:1010 random decoy IPs
--source-port 53spoof source port (DNS)
--data-length 50pad packets to avoid signatures
--spoof-mac 0randomize MAC address
--randomize-hostsshuffle target order
Common Workflows
Full TCP scan with version & default scripts
nmap -sS -sV -sC -p- -T4 -oA full 10.10.10.10
UDP top 100 (slow — be patient)
nmap -sU --top-ports 100 -T4 -oN udp.txt 10.10.10.10
Vulnerability scan on discovered services
nmap -sV --script vuln -p 80,443,445 10.10.10.10
Stealthy scan with decoys + timing
nmap -sS -T2 -f -D RND:5 --source-port 53 -p 22,80,443 10.10.10.10
cherry root › cheatsheet › Burp Suite
Burp Suite Cheatsheet
Tools, payload types, extensions, shortcuts.
Proxy
Intercept on/offcapture requests before they leave
Target > Scoperestrict to target — silences noise
HTTP historyfilter by scope, status, MIME
Dropdiscard intercepted request
Forwardsend request through unchanged
Repeater
Ctrl+Rsend current request to Repeater
Ctrl+Spacesend the request
Tabskeep multiple variations side-by-side
Compareright-click → send responses to Comparer
Intruder — Attack Types
Sniperone payload set, one position at a time
Battering ramsame payload in all positions
Pitchforkparallel sets — same index per position
Cluster bombcartesian product — every combination
Intruder — Payloads
Simple listpaste / load from file
Brute forcercharset + length range
Numbersfrom/to/step — IDOR style
Custom iteratortemplated combinations
Grep — Matchflag responses containing success/error string
Decoder
URLencode/decode %xx
HTML&amp; entities
Base64JWT payloads, tokens
Hex / ASCIIraw bytes
HashMD5, SHA1, SHA256 on the fly
Comparer & Scanner
Comparerdiff two requests or responses — word/byte
Scanner (Pro)passive on every request, active on demand
Scan profilecrawl + audit, light, deep, custom
Essential Extensions (BApp)
Logger++grep across all traffic
AutorizeauthZ testing — replay as another user
Turbo Intruderhigh-throughput, race conditions
Param Minerhidden parameter / header discovery
JWT Editordecode + sign JWTs
Keyboard Shortcuts
Ctrl+Rsend to Repeater
Ctrl+Isend to Intruder
Ctrl+Bforward intercept (also B in proxy)
Ctrl+Fforward
Ctrl+Ttoggle intercept on/off
Ctrl+UURL-decode selection
Tips
Scope hygiene saves hours — set it before you start clicking.
Target > Scope > Add > target.com  (+ Use advanced control)
Auto-add to scope when you hit "Add to scope" from Target tab.
Hotkey "Forward intercept and don't intercept HTTP responses" lets you breeze past static assets.
cherry root › cheatsheet › curl
curl Cheatsheet
The Swiss army knife of HTTP.
Basic
curl URLGET, body to stdout
-o filesave to specific file
-Osave with remote filename
-Lfollow redirects
-IHEAD only — see response headers
-iinclude response headers in body output
-v / -vvverbose — see request + response
-ssilent (no progress bar)
Methods
-X POSTchange method
-X PUTupsert
-X DELETEremove resource
-X PATCHpartial update
Headers
-H "Content-Type: application/json"JSON body
-H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN"bearer auth
-H "Cookie: a=1; b=2"manual cookies
-A "Mozilla/5.0..."User-Agent shortcut
-e "https://ref"Referer shortcut
Data
-d "name=val"URL-encoded POST body
-d @file.jsonread body from file
--data-urlencodeforce URL encoding
-F "file=@photo.jpg"multipart upload
-F "k=v"multipart form field
Auth & Cookies
-u user:passBasic auth
--cookie-jar c.txtsave received cookies
--cookie c.txtsend cookies from file
--digestuse Digest auth
SSL / TLS
-kignore cert errors
--cacert ca.pemtrust specific CA
--cert client.pemmTLS client cert
--tlsv1.2force TLS 1.2
--resolve host:port:IPoverride DNS
Output / Timing
-w "%{http_code}\n"format output (status code, time, size...)
--max-time 10total timeout (s)
--connect-timeout 5connect-phase timeout
-w "%{time_total}"measure total request time
Common Workflows
POST JSON to an API
curl -X POST https://api.x/v1/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"user":"a","pass":"b"}'
Login & reuse session cookie
curl -c jar.txt -d "u=a&p=b" https://site/login
curl -b jar.txt https://site/dashboard
Upload a file (multipart)
curl -F "file=@report.pdf" -F "tag=quarter" https://site/upload
Follow redirect chain verbosely
curl -L -v -o /dev/null https://short.url/abc
Test specific TLS version
curl --tlsv1.2 -v https://site 2>&1 | grep -i "SSL connection"
cherry root › cheatsheet › grep
grep Cheatsheet
Search text fast — flags, regex flavours, common patterns.
Basic Flags
-icase insensitive
-vinvert — non-matching lines
-r / -Rrecursive into directories
-nshow line number
-lfilenames only (with match)
-Lfilenames only (without match)
-ccount matches per file
-wwhole word match
-xwhole line match
-oprint only matched portion
Pattern Type
-Eextended regex (egrep)
-Ffixed string — fastest (fgrep)
-PPerl regex — full PCRE
-Gbasic regex (default)
Context
-A 33 lines after match
-B 33 lines before
-C 33 lines around (both)
Multi-Pattern
-e "a" -e "b"multiple patterns
grep -E "a|b"alternation
-f patterns.txtpatterns from file (one per line)
Common Pipes
grep X | grep -v Yfilter then exclude
grep X | awk '{print $1}'extract first column
... | sort | uniq -c | sort -rncount + rank
grep --include="*.php" -rlimit to file type
Regex Cheats
\ddigit (PCRE)
\wword char (PCRE)
\swhitespace (PCRE)
^startline begins with
end$line ends with
[abc]character class
[^abc]negated class
{2,4}2 to 4 repetitions (-E)
(group)capturing group (-E or -P)
Practical Examples
Find IPv4 addresses in a file
grep -Eo "([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}" access.log
Find email addresses
grep -Eio "[a-z0-9._%+-]+@[a-z0-9.-]+\.[a-z]{2,}" leak.txt
Errors in the last 100 log lines
tail -n 100 /var/log/syslog | grep -i error
Find secrets in source — case-insensitive, recursive
grep -rniE "(api[_-]?key|secret|password|token)\s*=" .
Top 10 IPs hitting your site
grep -Eo "([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}" access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
cherry root › cheatsheet › git
git Cheatsheet
For developers and red-teamers — including .git/ exposure attacks.
Setup
git config --global user.name "X"set author name
git config --global user.email a@bset author email
git config --global init.defaultBranch maindefault branch name
Basics
git initnew repo here
git clone URLcopy remote repo
git statuscurrent state
git add filestage changes
git commit -m "msg"commit staged
git push / pull / fetchsync with remote
Branches
git branchlist local branches
git checkout -b newcreate + switch
git switch mainswitch (modern)
git merge othermerge other → current
git rebase mainreplay commits on main
git cherry-pick HASHapply one commit here
git branch -d namedelete merged branch
History
git log --oneline --graphvisual history
git log -pshow diffs
git log --allinclude all refs
git show HASHinspect one commit
git blame filewho last changed each line
git refloglocal HEAD history (recover lost commits)
Undo
git restore filediscard unstaged changes
git reset fileunstage
git reset --hard HASHmove HEAD, wipe working tree
git revert HASHnew commit that undoes another
git commit --amendedit last commit
Stash & Remote
git stashshelve changes
git stash poprestore most recent
git stash list / applymanage stashes
git remote -vlist remotes
git remote add origin URLattach remote
git push -u origin mainpush + set upstream
For Hackers
git log --all --source -- filefind a file across all branches/history
git log -S "password"find commits that add/remove a string
git secrets / gitleaksscan for keys, AWS creds, tokens
trufflehog git URLentropy-based secret hunt
gitroborg-wide leaked-secret scanner
.git/ Exposure on the Web
Classic misconfig: web server serves .git/ directory contents. Full history can be dumped → leaked credentials, hardcoded keys, internal source code.
# detect
curl -s https://target/.git/HEAD
# expected: "ref: refs/heads/main"

# dump
git-dumper https://target/.git/ ./loot
# or
wget -r --no-parent https://target/.git/

# then grep history for secrets
cd loot && git log -p | grep -iE "password|api[_-]?key|secret"
Even when directory listing is off, the structure is predictable — tools brute-force expected paths (HEAD, refs/, objects/...).
cherry root › cheatsheet › ports
Common Ports
Well-known ports grouped by category — service, protocol, attack relevance.
File Transfer
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
20 / 21 TCPFTP (data/control)Plaintext credentials — check anonymous login
69 UDPTFTPNo auth — config files, firmware images
115 TCPSFTP (legacy)Rare — modern SFTP uses port 22
873 TCPrsyncOpen modules → arbitrary file read/write
2049 TCPNFSCheck exports — world-readable shares common
Remote Access
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
22 TCPSSHBrute-force via Hydra; check for weak keys
23 TCPTelnetPlaintext — deprecated, default creds common on IoT
512-514 TCPrsh / rlogin / rexecLegacy — trust-based auth
1723 TCPPPTP VPNWeak — MS-CHAPv2 crackable
3389 TCPRDPBlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708); brute-force; NLA check
5900 TCPVNCWeak or no auth — try unauth first
5985 / 5986 TCPWinRM (HTTP/HTTPS)PowerShell remoting — psexec alternative
Web
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
80 TCPHTTPPrimary web target
443 TCPHTTPSTLS-wrapped web — check cert, ciphers
3000 TCPDev frameworksNode, Rails, Grafana — often left exposed
3128 TCPSquid proxyOpen proxy → internal pivot
8000 / 8080 / 8443HTTP / HTTPS altAdmin UIs, tomcat, jenkins
8888 TCPWeb admin / JupyterNotebook servers often without auth
9000 TCPPHP-FPM / SonarQubeFPM exposed → RCE
Mail
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
25 TCPSMTPUser enum via VRFY/EXPN; open relay test
110 TCPPOP3Email retrieval, plaintext
143 TCPIMAPEmail retrieval, plaintext
465 TCPSMTPSSMTP over TLS
587 TCPSMTP submissionAuthenticated mail send
993 TCPIMAPSIMAP over TLS
995 TCPPOP3SPOP3 over TLS
Database
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
1433 TCPMSSQLsqlmap target; xp_cmdshell → RCE
1521 TCPOracle DBTNS poisoning; default creds (sys/oracle)
3306 TCPMySQL / MariaDBBrute-force; UDF for RCE
5432 TCPPostgreSQLCOPY PROGRAM → RCE in older versions
5984 TCPCouchDBOften no auth; CVE-2017-12635
6379 TCPRedisDefault no auth → RCE via config rewrite (write SSH key)
9200 TCPElasticsearchOften unauthenticated — full data dump
11211 TCP/UDPMemcachedUDP amplification DDoS
27017 TCPMongoDBDefault no auth in older versions
50070 TCPHadoop NameNodeWeb UI — often no auth
Directory & Auth
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
88 TCP/UDPKerberosAS-REP roasting, Kerberoasting
389 TCPLDAPAnonymous bind → user enum
636 TCPLDAPSLDAP over TLS
3268 TCPLDAP Global CatalogForest-wide AD queries
Windows / SMB
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
135 TCPMSRPCWindows RPC endpoint mapper
137-139 UDP/TCPNetBIOSName service / session — SMB enumeration
445 TCPSMB (direct)EternalBlue, file shares, null sessions
Routing & Network
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
53 TCP/UDPDNSZone transfers (AXFR), DNS tunneling, cache poisoning
67 / 68 UDPDHCP (server/client)Rogue DHCP attacks
111 TCP/UDPRPCbind / portmapperUsed by NFS — enumerate via rpcinfo
123 UDPNTPMonlist amplification DDoS
161 / 162 UDPSNMP (poll/trap)"public" community → full device inventory
179 TCPBGPRouting protocol — peer hijack
1080 TCPSOCKS proxyOften open as pivot
Monitoring & Other
PortServiceNotes / Attack Relevance
514 UDPsyslogLog forwarding — spoofable
631 TCPIPP / CUPSPrinters — often vulnerable
2375 / 2376 TCPDocker APIExposed → container escape → host RCE
4444 TCPMetasploit defaultCommon reverse shell handler
5601 TCPKibanaElasticsearch UI — auth often weak
cherry root › references › comparison
Protocols & Ports
Common protocols side by side — port, transport, encryption and attack surface.
PortProtocolTransportEncrypted?Common UseAttack Surface
20/21FTPTCPNoFile transferCleartext creds, anon login, bounce
22SSHTCPYesRemote shell / SFTPBrute force, weak keys, old ciphers
22SFTPTCPYesFile transfer over SSHInherits SSH exposure
23TelnetTCPNoLegacy remote shellCleartext creds, full session sniffing
25SMTPTCPNo*Mail sendingOpen relay, spoofing, user enum
53DNSUDP/TCPNoName resolutionSpoofing, cache poison, tunneling, AXFR
80HTTPTCPNoWeb pagesInjection, MITM, full app attack surface
110POP3TCPNo*Mail retrievalCleartext creds, sniffing
123NTPUDPNoTime syncAmplification DDoS, spoofing
143IMAPTCPNo*Mail retrievalCleartext creds, sniffing
161SNMPUDPNoDevice managementDefault community strings, info leak
389LDAPTCP/UDPNoDirectory servicesAnon bind, injection, enum
443HTTPSTCPYesEncrypted webWeak TLS, app-layer attacks, cert issues
445SMBTCPNo*File / printer sharingEternalBlue, null sessions, relay
636LDAPSTCPYesEncrypted directoryWeak TLS, enum if bound
1433MSSQLTCPNo*Microsoft SQL DBBrute force, xp_cmdshell, injection
3306MySQLTCPNo*MySQL databaseBrute force, weak creds, injection
3389RDPTCPYesRemote desktopBlueKeep, brute force, MITM
5900VNCTCPNo*Remote desktopNo/weak auth, sniffing
Defensive Layers · IDS vs IPS vs WAF vs Firewall vs EDR
LayerDetectsBlocksScopeExample
FirewallPorts / IPs / rulesYesNetwork perimeter (L3/L4)iptables, pfSense
IDSSuspicious traffic / signaturesNo (alerts only)Network / host monitoringSnort, Zeek
IPSSuspicious traffic / signaturesYes (inline)Inline network trafficSuricata, Snort inline
WAFWeb app attacks (L7)YesHTTP/HTTPS application layerModSecurity, Cloudflare
EDREndpoint behavior / malwareYesHosts / endpointsCrowdStrike, Defender ATP
* = plaintext by default; may run over TLS (e.g. SMTPS 465, IMAPS 993, POP3S 995) or VPN.
